techniques.name: Desert Travel Strategy
techniques.subtitle: Move at night, shelter by day

steps.instruction: Travel only at night or during dawn/dusk.
steps.helpDetail: Midday desert temps can exceed 130°F at ground level. You'll lose 1-2 quarts of sweat per hour walking in heat. At night, temps drop 30-50°F.

steps.instruction: Stay covered — loose, light-colored clothing covering all skin.
steps.helpDetail: Exposed skin in direct sun dehydrates faster than covered skin. Arabic robes exist for a reason. Cover your head and neck.

steps.instruction: Navigate by stars at night, compass in day.
steps.helpDetail: Without landmarks, people walk in circles (dominant leg push). Use celestial navigation or a compass to maintain direction.

steps.instruction: Never walk without water. Ration sweat, not water.
steps.helpDetail: Desert survival without water: 2-3 days walking, 5-7 days resting in shade. Your water goes in you, not on you.

techniques.name: Desert Shade Shelter
techniques.subtitle: Double-layer shade with air gap

steps.instruction: Create a double-layer shade with 12-inch air gap.
steps.helpDetail: Two layers of material (tarp over tarp) with an air gap between them reduces ground-level temperature by 30-40°F.

steps.instruction: Raise the shelter 18 inches off the ground.
steps.helpDetail: Ground surface temperature can be 30°F hotter than air 18 inches up. A raised platform or elevated tarp helps.

steps.instruction: Orient the open side away from prevailing wind.
steps.helpDetail: Desert winds carry sand. Open side should face the direction that provides breeze without sandblasting.

steps.instruction: If no materials: dig a trench and cover with any available material.
steps.helpDetail: Dig 2 feet deep — subterranean temps are significantly cooler. Cover for shade. Even a backpack propped up provides head shade.

techniques.name: Finding Water in Desert
techniques.subtitle: Follow signs to hidden sources

steps.instruction: Look for green vegetation — especially cottonwood, willow, and palm.
steps.helpDetail: These trees require consistent water. Their roots reach groundwater. Dig at the base of these trees.

steps.instruction: Dig in the outside bend of dry washes and riverbeds.
steps.helpDetail: Water pools at the outer bend. Dig 1-3 feet. Let the hole fill over 30 minutes. If groundwater is there, it will seep in.

steps.instruction: Watch for birds (especially doves) at dusk — they fly toward water.
steps.helpDetail: Grain-eating birds need daily water. At dusk they fly in straight lines to water sources. Follow their direction.

steps.instruction: Check rock crevices and shaded north-facing cliff bases.
steps.helpDetail: Tinajas (natural rock pools) can hold water for weeks after rain. Check shaded depressions in rock.

techniques.name: Jungle Travel
techniques.subtitle: Follow water, machete your path

steps.instruction: Follow streams downstream — they lead to rivers, rivers lead to people.
steps.helpDetail: 99% of tropical settlements are on waterways. Walking along a stream also ensures water access.

steps.instruction: Travel on ridgelines when possible — less vegetation, better visibility.
steps.helpDetail: Valley floors have the densest vegetation. Ridges are drier and sparser. Navigation is easier with visibility.

steps.instruction: Mark your path — the jungle looks identical in every direction.
steps.helpDetail: Blaze trees (cut bark), break branches, tie cloth. Without markers you will loop back to where you started.

steps.instruction: Watch your feet and where you grab — snakes and insects are everywhere.
steps.helpDetail: Never put hands or feet where you can't see. Probe ahead with a stick. Shake out boots and clothing before wearing.

techniques.name: Jungle Hammock Shelter
techniques.subtitle: Sleep off the ground away from insects and flooding

steps.instruction: Sleep OFF the ground. Always.
steps.helpDetail: Ground in tropical environments = ants, scorpions, snakes, moisture, and fungal infections. Build a raised platform or improvised hammock.

steps.instruction: Use poncho or tarp as a rain fly above your sleeping area.
steps.helpDetail: It rains daily in the tropics. An overhead cover is critical. Angle it for runoff — water pooling causes collapse.

steps.instruction: Tuck mosquito netting under your sleeping surface.
steps.helpDetail: Malaria, dengue, Zika — all transmitted by night-biting mosquitoes. A net is possibly the single most important piece of jungle survival gear.

steps.instruction: Build a smudge fire nearby to drive away insects.
steps.helpDetail: Green wood and damp leaves create smoke. Keep the fire small and smoky. The smoke deters mosquitoes within a 10-foot radius.

techniques.name: Snow Shelter (Quinzhee)
techniques.subtitle: Pile, settle, hollow — warmer than outside

steps.instruction: Pile snow into a mound 7-8 feet tall, 10 feet wide.
steps.helpDetail: The bigger the pile, the more insulation. Mixing snow of different temperatures during piling helps it harden.

steps.instruction: Let the mound settle for 1-2 hours (sintering).
steps.helpDetail: Snow crystals bond together. This makes the structure strong enough to hollow out without collapsing. Critical step — don't skip.

steps.instruction: Insert sticks 12 inches deep all over the mound.
steps.helpDetail: These are thickness guides. When hollowing, stop when you hit sticks. This ensures uniform wall thickness.

steps.instruction: Hollow from a low entrance upward. Sleep platform above entrance.
steps.helpDetail: Cold air sinks. The entrance should be below the sleeping platform. Interior temp stays near 32°F even when it's -40°F outside.

steps.instruction: Poke a fist-sized ventilation hole in the roof.
steps.helpDetail: Carbon dioxide from breathing accumulates. Without ventilation, you'll suffocate. Check the hole isn't blocked by new snowfall.

techniques.name: Arctic Travel & Navigation
techniques.subtitle: Energy conservation is everything

steps.instruction: Travel during warmest hours. Shelter during extreme cold.
steps.helpDetail: Unlike desert (travel at night), Arctic travel should occur during midday warmth. Nighttime temps drop to unsurvivable levels.

steps.instruction: Manage layers to prevent sweating.
steps.helpDetail: Sweating in cold = death. Wet clothing loses insulation. Remove layers BEFORE you overheat. Vent at neck and wrists.

steps.instruction: Use snowshoes or improvised floatation to prevent post-holing.
steps.helpDetail: Walking through deep snow without snowshoes burns 3× the calorie. Tie evergreen branches to feet for improvised snowshoes.

steps.instruction: Watch for thin ice: dark ice, snow-covered ice, and ice near moving water.
steps.helpDetail: Clear blue ice = strong. White/opaque ice = weaker (air bubbles). Snow masks thin spots. Near inlets/outlets ice is always thinner.

techniques.name: Frostbite Prevention & Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Frozen tissue dies — prevent at all costs

steps.instruction: Recognize: white/gray waxy skin, numbness, hardness.
steps.helpDetail: Frostbite progresses: frostnip (red, painful) → superficial (blistered, numb) → deep (hard, frozen solid). Early stages are fully reversible.

steps.instruction: Rewarm in body heat: armpit, groin, stomach.
steps.helpDetail: Place frostbitten fingers in your armpits. Place frostbitten toes against a companion's stomach. Skin-to-skin contact is ideal.

steps.instruction: If possible, rewarm in 98-102°F water for 20-30 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: NOT hot water — lukewarm. Rewarming is extremely painful. Give pain medication first if available. Do NOT rub or massage.

steps.instruction: Do NOT rewarm if refreezing is possible.
steps.helpDetail: Thawed-then-refrozen tissue suffers far worse damage than tissue left frozen. If you can't maintain warmth, leave it frozen until you reach help.

techniques.name: Lightning Safety in Mountains
techniques.subtitle: Get low, get small, get separate

steps.instruction: Get below treeline and away from peaks, ridges, and isolated trees.
steps.helpDetail: Lightning strikes the highest point. If you're on a ridge, you ARE the highest point. Descend immediately when storms approach.

steps.instruction: Assume the lightning crouch: squat on balls of feet, arms around knees.
steps.helpDetail: Minimize ground contact. Ground current from nearby strikes kills more people than direct hits. The crouch keeps you small and reduces conduction path.

steps.instruction: Stay away from water, metal, and wet rock.
steps.helpDetail: Water conducts electricity. Metal (trekking poles, camp stoves) attracts nothing but conducts well. Ditch metal and move away from lakes/streams.

steps.instruction: Groups should spread 50+ feet apart.
steps.helpDetail: Ground current from a nearby strike can hit everyone if huddled together. Spread out so a single strike doesn't incapacitate all members.

techniques.name: Mountain Altitude Management
techniques.subtitle: Climb high, sleep low

steps.instruction: Above 8,000 ft: ascend no more than 1,000 ft per day.
steps.helpDetail: Your body needs time to increase red blood cell production. Ascending too fast outpaces adaptation.

steps.instruction: Climb high, sleep low.
steps.helpDetail: It's OK to climb 2,000 ft during the day for acclimatization, but return to lower camp to sleep. Your body acclimatizes best during rest at lower altitudes.

steps.instruction: Hydrate aggressively — 3-4 liters per day at altitude.
steps.helpDetail: Altitude increases fluid loss through respiration (drier air, faster breathing). Dehydration worsens altitude sickness.

steps.instruction: Golden rule: if symptoms worsen, descend immediately.
steps.helpDetail: AMS → HACE or HAPE can progress in hours. Altitude illness has one treatment: lower altitude. Everything else is a stopgap.

techniques.name: Open Water Survival
techniques.subtitle: Float, signal, conserve

steps.instruction: Stay with the vessel or debris as long as possible.
steps.helpDetail: A boat, even capsized, is visible to rescuers. You are nearly invisible in open water. Climb on any floating debris.

steps.instruction: HELP position: Heat Escape Lessening Position.
steps.helpDetail: Cross arms over chest, pull knees to chest. This protects the three highest heat loss areas: head, armpits, groin. Reduces heat loss by 50%.

steps.instruction: Signal with mirror, dye marker, whistle, or bright clothing.
steps.helpDetail: A signal mirror is visible for 50+ miles. Wave bright clothing. Use any dye to color the water around you for aerial detection.

steps.instruction: Never drink seawater. Collect rain at every opportunity.
steps.helpDetail: Use any fabric, life raft surfaces, or clothing to channel rainwater into containers. Your survival depends entirely on fresh water.

techniques.name: Urban Disaster Survival
techniques.subtitle: Earthquake, building collapse, civil unrest

steps.instruction: Earthquake: DROP, COVER, HOLD ON.
steps.helpDetail: Under a sturdy desk or table. Away from windows and exterior walls. In bed: stay there, cover head with pillow. Doorways are NOT safer.

steps.instruction: After shaking stops: check for gas leaks, structural damage.
steps.helpDetail: If you smell gas, open windows and leave. Do not use flames or electrical switches. Check for cracks in walls and foundation.

steps.instruction: Building collapse: protect airways, tap on pipes to signal.
steps.helpDetail: Cover nose and mouth with fabric. Do not use matches (gas leaks). Tap SOS on pipes or walls — sound travels through structure. Conserve energy.

steps.instruction: Civil unrest: stay indoors, fill bathtubs with water, secure doors.
steps.helpDetail: Water supply may be disrupted. Fill every container. Stay away from windows. Identify multiple exit routes from your building.

techniques.name: Swamp & Wetland Survival
techniques.subtitle: Navigate insects, mud, and hidden dangers

steps.instruction: Test each step with a pole before committing weight.
steps.helpDetail: Probe ahead. If sinks more than knee-deep, reroute. Travel on game trails.

steps.instruction: If stuck in mud: lean forward and swim/crawl out.
steps.helpDetail: Struggling creates suction. Lean forward to distribute weight. Remove pack first.

steps.instruction: Protect against insects: mud coating, smoke, long sleeves.
steps.helpDetail: Swamp insects carry disease. Cover skin with mud. Stay near smoky fire.

steps.instruction: Water is abundant but contaminated—always purify.
steps.helpDetail: Boil, filter, or use tablets. Never drink swamp water untreated.

techniques.name: Coastal & Tidal Survival
techniques.subtitle: Tides, rip currents, shore foraging

steps.instruction: Learn tide cycle—changes every ~6 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Note high water debris line on beach. Camp ABOVE it.

steps.instruction: Rip current: swim PARALLEL to shore, not against it.
steps.helpDetail: Rip currents pull straight out. Swim sideways until free.

steps.instruction: Forage at low tide: tidal pools have shellfish, crabs, seaweed.
steps.helpDetail: Mussels, clams, limpets, snails—all edible when cooked.

steps.instruction: Signal from headlands—most visible from sea and air.
steps.helpDetail: Build signal fires on high points. Lay SOS in rocks on beach.

techniques.name: Earthquake Survival
techniques.subtitle: Drop, Cover, Hold On — then assess

steps.instruction: DROP to hands and knees. COVER under furniture. HOLD ON.
steps.helpDetail: Under desk or table. If nothing, interior wall. Cover head.

steps.instruction: Outdoors: move to open area away from buildings and lines.
steps.helpDetail: Falling debris kills. Get away from walls and trees.

steps.instruction: After shaking: expect aftershocks. Check for injuries.
steps.helpDetail: Aftershocks can be strong. Stay alert for hours.

steps.instruction: Check for gas leaks, structural damage, fire hazards.
steps.helpDetail: Gas smell = leave immediately. No flames or switches.

techniques.name: Tornado Survival
techniques.subtitle: Get underground or lowest interior room

steps.instruction: Get to lowest floor. Basement ideal. Interior room.
steps.helpDetail: Bathroom, closet, hallway. Stay away from windows.

steps.instruction: Under sturdy furniture. Cover head with arms or mattress.
steps.helpDetail: Flying debris kills, not wind. Protect head and vitals.

steps.instruction: Outdoors: lie flat in ditch, cover head.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT shelter under overpasses. Ditch is safer.

steps.instruction: Vehicle: drive perpendicular to path or park, duck, seatbelt.
steps.helpDetail: Do not outrun. Park, duck below windows, cover with blanket.

techniques.name: Wildfire Survival
techniques.subtitle: Escape downhill, into wind, to burned zones

steps.instruction: Move downhill and into the wind.
steps.helpDetail: Fire travels uphill faster. Already-burned areas are safe zones.

steps.instruction: Cover mouth with wet cloth. Stay low.
steps.helpDetail: Smoke inhalation kills faster than burns. Crawl if thick.

steps.instruction: Go to water, roads, cleared areas, or burned zones.
steps.helpDetail: Rivers, roads, parking lots are firebreaks.

steps.instruction: Trapped: clear vegetation, lie face down in depression.
steps.helpDetail: Clear 10-foot radius. Lie face down. Breathe ground-level air.

techniques.name: Avalanche Survival
techniques.subtitle: Swim, air pocket, conserve oxygen

steps.instruction: If caught: swim aggressively to stay on surface.
steps.helpDetail: Backstroke motion. Ditch heavy gear—it drags you down.

steps.instruction: As it slows: cup hands over face for air pocket.
steps.helpDetail: This is your lifeline. 15-30 minutes of air.

steps.instruction: When stopped: spit to determine which way is up.
steps.helpDetail: Gravity pulls saliva down. Dig upward.

steps.instruction: Push one arm up. Stay calm — panic uses 3x oxygen.
steps.helpDetail: If near surface, hand may break through for rescuers.

techniques.name: Quicksand Escape
techniques.subtitle: You will NOT sink completely — stay calm

steps.instruction: Stop moving. Do NOT struggle or kick.
steps.helpDetail: Quicksand is denser than body. Max waist-deep. Struggling worsens.

steps.instruction: Drop pack and heavy items.
steps.helpDetail: Extra weight pushes deeper.

steps.instruction: Lean back slowly. Float on your back.
steps.helpDetail: Spread arms wide. Increases surface area.

steps.instruction: Wiggle legs to break suction. Pull out one at a time.
steps.helpDetail: Small movements. Pull at angle, not straight up. Slow wins.

techniques.name: Lightning Safety
techniques.subtitle: Get low, get small, avoid tall objects

steps.instruction: Get off ridgelines, peaks, high ground immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Lightning strikes highest point. Descend if possible.

steps.instruction: Avoid tall trees, metal fences, water.
steps.helpDetail: All excellent conductors. Group of short trees safer than one tall.

steps.instruction: Lightning crouch: balls of feet, heels together, squat, ears covered.
steps.helpDetail: Minimizes ground contact. Heels together routes current around body.

steps.instruction: Strike victim is safe to touch. Begin CPR if needed.
steps.helpDetail: No charge remains. Check breathing and pulse immediately.

techniques.name: Tsunami Response
techniques.subtitle: Recognize warning signs and reach high ground

steps.instruction: If you feel a strong earthquake near the coast, move to high ground immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Do not wait for official warnings. A strong coastal earthquake IS the warning. You may have only 10-15 minutes before the first wave.

steps.instruction: Watch for natural warning signs: ocean suddenly pulling far from shore.
steps.helpDetail: If the ocean recedes dramatically, exposing the sea floor, a tsunami is incoming. This is the ocean 'drawing back' before the wave. RUN.

steps.instruction: Move inland or to elevation above 100 feet. Go UP, not just away.
steps.helpDetail: Tsunamis can travel miles inland on flat ground. Elevation is your only protection. Upper floors of concrete buildings work if no high ground exists.

steps.instruction: Stay at high ground for hours. Tsunamis come in multiple waves.
steps.helpDetail: The first wave is often NOT the largest. Waves can continue for 8-12 hours. Wait for official all-clear before returning to low ground.

techniques.name: Tornado Shelter Procedure
techniques.subtitle: Protect yourself from tornado winds and debris

steps.instruction: Get to the lowest interior room away from windows.
steps.helpDetail: Basement is best. If no basement: interior closet, bathroom, or hallway on the lowest floor. Stay away from windows, doors, and exterior walls.

steps.instruction: Get under a sturdy table and cover your head and neck.
steps.helpDetail: Flying debris causes most tornado injuries and deaths. Use pillows, mattresses, blankets, or your arms to protect your head and neck.

steps.instruction: If outdoors, lie flat in the nearest ditch or low spot.
steps.helpDetail: Cover your head with your arms. Do NOT try to outrun a tornado in a car. If caught driving, park, keep your seatbelt on, and duck below window level.

steps.instruction: After the tornado passes, watch for downed power lines and gas leaks.
steps.helpDetail: Do not enter damaged buildings. Smell for gas. Avoid downed wires. Check on neighbors, especially elderly and disabled individuals.

techniques.name: Wildfire Evacuation
techniques.subtitle: Survive and escape an approaching wildfire

steps.instruction: Evacuate EARLY. Do not wait for mandatory evacuation orders.
steps.helpDetail: Wildfires can move 6-14 mph in forest and 25+ mph in grassland. Embers travel miles ahead of the fire front. By the time you see flames, you may have minutes.

steps.instruction: Drive with headlights on, windows up, vents closed.
steps.helpDetail: Smoke reduces visibility to near zero. Drive slowly. If road is blocked, park in a clear area, turn off the engine, close all windows, lie on the floor below window level.

steps.instruction: If trapped, find a body of water, cleared area, or road.
steps.helpDetail: Roads, parking lots, and rocky areas have no fuel for fire. A pond or stream provides protection. Lie face-down in shallow water or cover yourself with soil.

steps.instruction: Cover exposed skin and breathe through a wet cloth.
steps.helpDetail: Radiant heat can burn skin without direct flame contact. A wet bandana over nose and mouth filters some smoke particles. Synthetic clothing melts — natural fibers are safer.

techniques.name: Earthquake: Indoor Response
techniques.subtitle: Drop, cover, and hold on

steps.instruction: DROP to your hands and knees immediately.
steps.helpDetail: This position prevents you from being knocked down by shaking. It also allows you to crawl to cover. Do NOT try to run outside — falling debris is the greatest danger.

steps.instruction: Take COVER under a sturdy desk or table. Protect your head and neck.
steps.helpDetail: If no table is available, get against an interior wall and cover your head with your arms. Stay away from windows, shelves, and heavy objects.

steps.instruction: HOLD ON to your shelter until the shaking stops.
steps.helpDetail: Strong earthquakes can last 30+ seconds. Aftershocks may follow within minutes. If your cover moves, move with it. Stay covered.

steps.instruction: After shaking stops, check for injuries and exit the building carefully.
steps.helpDetail: Use stairs, never elevators. Check for gas leaks. Be prepared for aftershocks. Turn off utilities if you suspect damage. Do not re-enter damaged buildings.

techniques.name: Earthquake: Outdoor Response
techniques.subtitle: Stay in the open away from structures

steps.instruction: Move to an open area away from buildings, trees, and power lines.
steps.helpDetail: Building facades, glass, and bricks fall outward during earthquakes. Get at least one building-height away from structures.

steps.instruction: Drop to the ground and protect your head.
steps.helpDetail: Even outdoors, debris can fly. Cover your head with your arms or any available protection. Stay low and stable.

steps.instruction: If driving, pull over to a clear area. Stay in the car.
steps.helpDetail: Avoid stopping under bridges, overpasses, or near buildings. The car provides protection from falling debris. Set the parking brake.

techniques.name: Lightning Crouch Position
techniques.subtitle: Minimize your profile during a lightning storm

steps.instruction: Get off high ground, ridges, and exposed areas.
steps.helpDetail: Lightning strikes the highest point. Get below the tree line if on a mountain. Avoid open fields, water, and isolated tall trees.

steps.instruction: Crouch low with feet together, head tucked, hands over ears.
steps.helpDetail: The "lightning crouch": minimize contact with ground and height simultaneously. Feet together reduces the voltage difference across your body if ground current strikes nearby.

steps.instruction: If in a group, spread out at least 50 feet apart.
steps.helpDetail: This prevents a single strike from hitting multiple people via ground current. If someone is struck, others can provide aid immediately.

techniques.name: Volcanic Ashfall Protection
techniques.subtitle: Survive falling volcanic ash and debris

steps.instruction: Get indoors and seal windows and doors.
steps.helpDetail: Volcanic ash is not like wood ash — it's pulverized rock and glass. Breathing it causes severe lung damage. Seal gaps with wet towels or tape.

steps.instruction: Wear an N95 mask or breathe through a damp cloth if outdoors.
steps.helpDetail: Ash particles are tiny and razor-sharp. They slice lung tissue. Any filtration is better than none. Goggles protect eyes from abrasion.

steps.instruction: Clear ash from roofs — wet ash can collapse structures.
steps.helpDetail: Volcanic ash is extremely heavy, especially when wet. As little as 4 inches of wet ash can collapse a roof. Remove it from the roof when safe to do so.

steps.instruction: Avoid driving. Ash destroys engines and blocks visibility.
steps.helpDetail: Ash clogs air filters, abrades moving parts, and turns roads into slippery surfaces. Drive only if evacuating, at very low speed with lights on.

techniques.name: Jungle Movement & Navigation
techniques.subtitle: Navigate dense tropical forest without trails

steps.instruction: Follow streams and rivers — they're natural highways in dense jungle.
steps.helpDetail: Dense jungle off-trail movement is ~0.5 miles/hour. Along waterways: 1-2 miles/hour. Streams lead to rivers, rivers lead to settlements. Stay near the bank, not in the water (parasites, current).

steps.instruction: Use a machete or heavy stick to probe ahead. Watch for snakes.
steps.helpDetail: Prod vegetation before stepping. Many venomous snakes are camouflaged in leaf litter. Step ON logs, not over them — snakes rest on the far side. Always look before grabbing branches or vines.

steps.instruction: Travel early morning and late afternoon. Rest during midday heat.
steps.helpDetail: Midday jungle heat + humidity = dangerous core temperature rise. Travel 0600-1000 and 1500-1800. Rest during the hottest hours. Dehydration rate in tropical jungle: 1-2 liters per hour of heavy movement.

techniques.name: Swamp Navigation & Safety
techniques.subtitle: Move safely through wetland environments

steps.instruction: Test ground before stepping. Use a long pole to probe depth and firmness.
steps.helpDetail: Swamp mud can be waist-deep. Probe every step with a 6-foot pole. Solid ground often follows tree root systems. Step on root mats when possible. If you start sinking, lean forward and 'swim' to firmer ground.

steps.instruction: Follow animal trails — they use the firmest paths.
steps.helpDetail: Deer, hogs, and other animals know the solid ground. Follow their trails. Animal trails also lead to water sources and higher ground. If a trail dead-ends at water, look for where it resumes on the other side.

steps.instruction: Protect against mosquitoes, leeches, and waterborne parasites continuously.
steps.helpDetail: Swamp hazards: mosquitoes (malaria, dengue), leeches (infection risk), parasitic worms in water (enter through skin). Cover all exposed skin. Apply mud as insect barrier. Check for leeches every hour.

techniques.name: High Altitude Sickness Management
techniques.subtitle: Recognize and treat altitude-related illness

steps.instruction: Recognize symptoms: headache, nausea, fatigue, dizziness above 8,000 feet.
steps.helpDetail: Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS) affects most people above 8,000 feet. Symptoms start 6-12 hours after ascending. The ONLY reliable cure is descending. Do NOT ignore symptoms — AMS can progress to fatal brain or lung swelling.

steps.instruction: Descend immediately if symptoms worsen or confusion develops.
steps.helpDetail: Golden rule: if symptoms get worse at the same altitude, DESCEND. Even 1,000 feet of descent provides significant relief. HACE (brain swelling) and HAPE (lung fluid) kill within hours if untreated.

steps.instruction: Climb high, sleep low. Ascend no more than 1,000 feet per day above 10,000 feet.
steps.helpDetail: Acclimatization takes 1-3 days per 1,000 feet. You can climb higher during the day but should sleep at a lower elevation. Hydrate aggressively — dehydration accelerates AMS. Avoid alcohol and sleeping pills.

techniques.name: Coastal Storm Shelter Strategy
techniques.subtitle: Survive hurricanes, typhoons, and coastal storms

steps.instruction: Get to high ground immediately. Storm surge kills more than wind.
steps.helpDetail: Storm surge can push seawater 20+ feet above normal. Move to ground at least 30 feet above sea level and 2 miles from coast. Storm surge arrives fast — within minutes. Don't wait for official warnings.

steps.instruction: Shelter in the strongest structure available. Interior rooms, lowest floor.
steps.helpDetail: Avoid windows. Interior rooms without exterior walls. Bathroom is often strongest (small room, plumbing reinforcement). If in open, find a ravine or depression on the lee side of a hill.

steps.instruction: After the storm: beware downed power lines, contaminated water, and unstable structures.
steps.helpDetail: Post-storm drowning in flood water is common. Electricity in standing water is invisible and lethal. Don't drink tap water until cleared. Structural damage may cause delayed collapse. Stay alert for 48+ hours.

techniques.name: Cave Shelter & Safety
techniques.subtitle: Use caves for shelter while avoiding their hazards

steps.instruction: Check for animal inhabitants before entering. Look for tracks, droppings, bones.
steps.helpDetail: Bears, big cats, snakes, and bats use caves. Fresh droppings = occupied. Strong musky smell = recent large animal. Throw rocks inside and listen. Never enter a cave that smells of ammonia (bat guano — histoplasmosis risk).

steps.instruction: Check airflow. Stale or foul air means poor ventilation — don't go deep.
steps.helpDetail: Caves can accumulate CO2, radon, and methane. If a flame burns dim or goes out, air is poor. Stay near the entrance where airflow is good. Never build a fire deep in a cave — smoke accumulation is lethal.

steps.instruction: Build fire near the entrance, not inside. Use cave for wind protection only.
steps.helpDetail: Fire at cave mouth provides warmth without smoke buildup. The cave walls reflect heat. Sleep between fire and back wall. Mark your entrance clearly if exploring deeper — cave disorientation is common and deadly.

techniques.name: Lightning Safety Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Minimize lightning strike risk during thunderstorms

steps.instruction: 30-30 Rule: if thunder follows lightning in less than 30 seconds, seek shelter.
steps.helpDetail: Each 5-second count between flash and thunder = 1 mile distance. 30 seconds = 6 miles = danger zone. Lightning can strike 10+ miles from its parent cloud. If you can hear thunder, you can be struck.

steps.instruction: Avoid: tall trees, hilltops, open fields, water, metal objects.
steps.helpDetail: Lightning hits the tallest object. Don't BE the tallest object. Avoid isolated trees — seek a uniform stand of short trees. Get off ridgelines. Leave water immediately. Remove metal frame backpacks.

steps.instruction: Lightning crouch: squat low, feet together, hands on knees, head down.
steps.helpDetail: If caught in the open: make yourself as small as possible while minimizing ground contact. Feet together reduces ground current risk. Don't lie flat — it increases ground current exposure. Space group members 50 feet apart.

techniques.name: Sandstorm Shelter-in-Place
techniques.subtitle: Protect yourself during a desert sandstorm

steps.instruction: Turn your back to the wind. Cover nose and mouth immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Sand particles cause severe lung damage. Wrap cloth tightly over nose and mouth — breathe through the fabric. Cover eyes completely — sand abrasion can blind you. Goggles or sunglasses help but wrap with cloth too.

steps.instruction: Get low. Find the lee side of a large rock, dune, or vehicle.
steps.helpDetail: Sand concentration is highest at 1-3 feet off the ground in moderate storms. Below that, and above 6 feet, concentration drops. Get behind a windbreak and sit with your back to the wind.

steps.instruction: STAY PUT until the storm passes. Mark your direction before visibility drops.
steps.helpDetail: Sandstorms reduce visibility to zero. Walking blind in desert = circling, falling into gullies, or walking the wrong direction. Before the storm hits, note your compass bearing and direction to shelter. Then wait it out.

techniques.name: Tundra & Arctic Travel
techniques.subtitle: Move safely in flat, featureless arctic environments

steps.instruction: Navigate by wind direction — prevailing winds are the most reliable compass in featureless terrain.
steps.helpDetail: Snow drifts, sastrugi (wind ridges), and vegetation lean indicate prevailing wind direction. Once established, use wind angle to maintain a bearing. Sun position helps when visible but arctic sun angles are extreme.

steps.instruction: Avoid thin ice: dark ice, snow-covered ice, ice near current or outflows.
steps.helpDetail: Clear blue ice 4+ inches thick supports a person. White/opaque ice is weaker. Snow-covered ice hides thickness. Stream inlets, outlets, and narrows have thinner ice. Spread your weight if unsure — crawl.

steps.instruction: Build snow walls or dig into snowdrifts for emergency shelter.
steps.helpDetail: Wind chill is the primary killer in tundra. A snow wall reduces wind by 70%. A snow trench takes 30 minutes to dig and provides immediate wind protection. Block the entrance and body heat warms the space above freezing.

techniques.name: Wildfire Entrapment Survival
techniques.subtitle: Survive when wildfire cuts off your escape

steps.instruction: Move to the largest burned area (black), a road, water body, or rocky area.
steps.helpDetail: Already-burned areas won't burn again. Large clearings and rock fields have no fuel. Water bodies provide heat protection. Roads create fuel breaks. Running uphill from fire is deadly — fire travels uphill 4x faster than on flat ground.

steps.instruction: If trapped: lie face-down in a depression, cover exposed skin.
steps.helpDetail: Clear vegetation around you in a 10-foot radius if possible. Lie in the lowest spot available. Cover your body with dirt. Breathe through cloth near the ground — superheated air rises. The fire front passes in 1-3 minutes.

steps.instruction: After the fire passes: watch for falling trees, hidden hot spots, and unstable ground.
steps.helpDetail: Burned trees fall without warning. Root systems burn underground creating hidden holes. Ash conceals hot spots that can cause severe burns. Move carefully with a probing stick. Watch for rolling rocks on burned slopes.

techniques.name: Hurricane Survival
techniques.subtitle: Survive high winds and storm surge

steps.instruction: Secure all windows/doors. Create a 'safe room' in interior.
steps.helpDetail: Board up windows if time. If not, stay in windowless room. Mattress over head protects from flying glass.

steps.instruction: Fill bathtubs and containers with water BEFORE storm hits.
steps.helpDetail: Municipal water often fails or gets contaminated. You need 1 gallon/person/day for at least 3 days.

steps.instruction: Stay inside during the 'eye' — calm is temporary.
steps.helpDetail: The eye passes in 10-30 mins. Winds return suddenly from opposite direction with full force. Don't go out.

techniques.name: Blizzard Survival
techniques.subtitle: Survive whiteout and extreme cold

steps.instruction: Shelter immediately. Do NOT try to walk for help in whiteout.
steps.helpDetail: You will get lost within 50 feet. Disorientation is total. Dig a snow trench if no structure available.

steps.instruction: If in car: run engine 10 mins/hour only. Clear exhaust pipe.
steps.helpDetail: Blocked exhaust kills via CO poisoning. Crack window slightly for air. Stay with vehicle.

steps.instruction: Exercise extremities to keep blood flowing. Don't sleep if shivering stops.
steps.helpDetail: stopping shivering is a sign of severe hypothermia. Move fingers/toes. Huddle for warmth.

techniques.name: Volcanic Ash Protection
techniques.subtitle: Breathing safety during eruption

steps.instruction: Cover mouth/nose with N95 mask or wet cloth.
steps.helpDetail: Volcanic ash is jagged glass, not dust. It shreds lungs. Wet cloth traps particles better than dry.

steps.instruction: Wear goggles. Do NOT wear contact lenses.
steps.helpDetail: Ash scratches corneas. Contacts trap ash against eye.

steps.instruction: Seal shelter. Tape doors/windows. Damp towels under doors.
steps.helpDetail: Ash is heavy and fine. It enters everywhere. Don't run AC/fans.

techniques.name: Lava & Pyroclastic Flow
techniques.subtitle: Evacuation from eruption zone

steps.instruction: Evacuate laterally (perpendicular) to lava flow.
steps.helpDetail: Lava follows gravity (valleys). Move to ridges/high ground. Don't try to outrun pyroclastic flow (travels 100mph+).

steps.instruction: Avoid low-lying areas (valleys, riverbeds).
steps.helpDetail: Lahars (mudflows) and lava channel into low spots. Stay high.

steps.instruction: Protect against heat radiation. Cover exposed skin.
steps.helpDetail: Radiant heat can burn from distance. Thick clothing (cotton/wool) protects against heat pulse.

techniques.name: Tsunami Survival
techniques.subtitle: Survive the wave train

steps.instruction: If you feel a quake or see water recede: RUN TO HIGH GROUND.
steps.helpDetail: Don't wait for sirens. Water sucking out to sea = wave incoming in minutes. Go 100ft high or 2 miles inland.

steps.instruction: If caught: climb a strong concrete building or tree.
steps.helpDetail: Wood houses float/crush. Reinforced concrete stands. Climb to 3rd floor or roof.

steps.instruction: Expect multiple waves. The first is rarely the largest.
steps.helpDetail: Waves arrive 10-60 mins apart. Stay high for HOURS. Water withdraws violently between waves.

techniques.name: Extreme Heat Management
techniques.subtitle: Prevent heat stroke in emergencies

steps.instruction: Stop all activity. Find or build shade.
steps.helpDetail: Direct sun adds 15-20°F to heat load. Dig to cooler soil if necessary.

steps.instruction: Wet skin/clothes if water available (not drinking water).
steps.helpDetail: Evaporation cools. Use urine/bad water on clothes (not skin if broken) to cool via evaporation.

steps.instruction: Elevate feet. Loosen clothing. Fan yourself.
steps.helpDetail: Promotes circulation. Heat exhaustion leads to stroke quickly. Monitor urine color (dark = danger).

techniques.name: Escaping Rip Current
techniques.subtitle: Swim out of ocean currents

steps.instruction: Do NOT swim against the current (toward shore). You will tire and drown.
steps.helpDetail: Rips move 5-8 mph. Olympic swimmers can't beat them. conserve energy.

steps.instruction: Swim parallel to shore (sideways) until out of the pull.
steps.helpDetail: Rips are narrow (20-100 ft). Swim sideways to escape the fast water channel.

steps.instruction: When current releases you, swim diagonally to shore.
steps.helpDetail: Waves help push you in. If tired, float to recover.

techniques.name: Self-Rescue from Ice
techniques.subtitle: Escape after falling through ice

steps.instruction: Control breathing. Don't panic (Cold Shock Response).
steps.helpDetail: You have 1 minute to control breathing, 10 minutes of movement before muscles fail. Use 1 minute to calm down.

steps.instruction: Kick feet up to float horizontal. Don't climb vertical.
steps.helpDetail: Horizontal position distributes weight. Kick hard to push chest onto ice edge.

steps.instruction: Pull with elbows/picks, kick like a seal. Roll away.
steps.helpDetail: Once on ice, ROLL away. Do not stand up until on thick ice/land.

techniques.name: Black Bear Encounter
techniques.subtitle: Dealing with black bears

steps.instruction: Stand your ground. Make noise. Look big.
steps.helpDetail: Black bears are timid. Wave arms. Yell 'Hey Bear'. Do NOT run.

steps.instruction: If it charges: stand ground (bluff charge).
steps.helpDetail: They often stop feet away. Spray bear spray if within 30 ft.

steps.instruction: If it attacks: FIGHT BACK vigorously.
steps.helpDetail: Punch eyes/nose. Use rocks/sticks. Do NOT play dead with black bears.

techniques.name: Grizzly/Brown Bear Encounter
techniques.subtitle: Dealing with brown bears

steps.instruction: Do NOT run. Back away slowly. Speak calmly.
steps.helpDetail: Don't look in eyes (aggression). Show you are human, not prey. Prepare spray.

steps.instruction: If it charges: STAND GROUND. Deploy spray.
steps.helpDetail: Running triggers predatory chase response. You cannot outrun a bear (35mph).

steps.instruction: If it makes contact: PLAY DEAD.
steps.helpDetail: Drop to stomach. Hands clasped behind neck. Spread legs (harder to flip). Stay still until bear leaves (20+ mins).

techniques.name: Polar Bear Encounter
techniques.subtitle: The only predator that actively hunts humans

steps.instruction: Deter immediately. Flare, warning shot, noise.
steps.helpDetail: Polar bears are curious/predatory. They don't bluff. If it comes toward you, it intends to eat you.

steps.instruction: Fight for your life. Use any weapon.
steps.helpDetail: Playing dead DOES NOT WORK — they will eat you. Lethal force is usually required.

techniques.name: Cougar/Mountain Lion
techniques.subtitle: Stalking predator defense

steps.instruction: Maintain eye contact. Do NOT turn back. Do NOT run.
steps.helpDetail: Cougars attack from behind/ambush. Stare them down.

steps.instruction: Look big. Raise jacket/arms. Pick up children.
steps.helpDetail: Convince cat you are a threat, not prey. Yell loudly.

steps.instruction: If attacked: Fight back. Protect neck.
steps.helpDetail: Use rocks, sticks, fists. Gouge eyes. They give up if prey fights too hard.

techniques.name: Wolf Pack Encounter
techniques.subtitle: Pack animal defense

steps.instruction: Stand ground. Do not run. Stare them down.
steps.helpDetail: Running triggers chase. Wolves test prey. Show aggression.

steps.instruction: Back away slowly while facing them. Keep group together.
steps.helpDetail: Defend the rear. Don't let them circle you.

steps.instruction: Use fire, smoke, or rocks to deter.
steps.helpDetail: Wolves fear fire. Throw rocks aggressively.

techniques.name: Moose/Elk Encounter
techniques.subtitle: Large herbivore defense

steps.instruction: Watch ears/hair. Ears back + hair up = charge.
steps.helpDetail: Moose attack more people than bears. Give wide berth.

steps.instruction: If charged: RUN. Get behind a tree.
steps.helpDetail: Unlike bears/cats, you CAN run from moose/elk (sort of). Put large tree between you.

steps.instruction: If knocked down: curl in ball. Protect head.
steps.helpDetail: They stomp. Stay ball until they leave.

techniques.name: Alligator/Croc Evasion
techniques.subtitle: Reptilian predator defense

steps.instruction: Stay 15ft from water edge. Don't wash/drink at same spot.
steps.helpDetail: They ambush from water edge. They are fast for 20 feet.

steps.instruction: Run in straight line away. Do NOT zigzag.
steps.helpDetail: Zigzag slows you down. Gators run straight. Outrun their sprint zone (30ft).

steps.instruction: If bitten: attack eyes and snout. Don't pull.
steps.helpDetail: Teeth curve back — pulling rips flesh. Poking eyes triggers release.

techniques.name: Tick Removal
techniques.subtitle: Safe removal to prevent disease

steps.instruction: Grasp tick close to skin with tweezers.
steps.helpDetail: Don't squeeze body (injects pathogens). Grab head.

steps.instruction: Pull upward with steady pressure. Don't twist.
steps.helpDetail: Twisting leaves mouthparts in skin. Pull straight.

steps.instruction: Clean area. Save tick if possible for ID.
steps.helpDetail: Wash with soap/water. Watch for bulls-eye rash (Lyme).

techniques.name: Shark Encounter
techniques.subtitle: Defense in water

steps.instruction: Maintain eye contact. Rotate as it circles.
steps.helpDetail: Sharks ambush. Knowing it's seen deters attack.

steps.instruction: Slow, rhythmic movements. Do not splash.
steps.helpDetail: Splashing = injured prey. Back away slowly to boat/shore.

steps.instruction: If attacked: hit nose, eyes, gills.
steps.helpDetail: Sensitive areas. Use camera, rock, or fist. Be aggressive.

techniques.name: Jellyfish Sting Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Neutralize venom

steps.instruction: Rinse with VINEGAR (or sea water). NOT fresh water.
steps.helpDetail: Fresh water triggers unfired nematocysts (stingers). Vinegar neutralizes them. Urine is a myth (often ineffective).

steps.instruction: Remove tentacles with tweezers/stick. Don't rub.
steps.helpDetail: Rubbing spreads toxin. Pluck carefully.

steps.instruction: Hot water soak (110°F) for pain.
steps.helpDetail: Heat breaks down protein venom. 20-45 minutes.

techniques.name: Monitoring Wildfire
techniques.subtitle: Watch for wind shifts and embers

steps.instruction: If fire is downwind: monitor wind direction constantly.
steps.helpDetail: Wind shifts are the #1 killer. If wind changes 180 degrees, the flank becomes the head and you are in the path.

steps.instruction: Clear 30 feet of flammable material around your location.
steps.helpDetail: Remove dry grass, wood piles, and furniture. Create a defensible space even if not evacuating yet.

steps.instruction: Watch for spot fires started by embers.
steps.helpDetail: Embers can travel up to a mile ahead of the fire front. Patrol your area and extinguish small starts immediately.

steps.instruction: Be ready to evacuate instantly if smoke thickens or wind changes.
steps.helpDetail: Have your 'go-bag' in the car. Back the car into the driveway for fast exit.

techniques.name: Trapped in Vehicle (Wildfire)
techniques.subtitle: Last resort shelter implementation

steps.instruction: Park in a clear area, away from heavy vegetation.
steps.helpDetail: Find a rocky outcrop, plowed field, or previously burned area. Avoid parking under trees or power lines.

steps.instruction: Close all windows and vents. Turn on AC to 'recirculate'.
steps.helpDetail: Keep smoke out. Recirculate mode prevents outside smoke from being drawn in.

steps.instruction: Lie on the floor below window level. Cover with wool blanket.
steps.helpDetail: Radiant heat passes through glass. The floor is the coolest spot. Wool protects against heat.

steps.instruction: Wait for the fire front to pass. Do not exit until safe.
steps.helpDetail: The main fire front often passes in 5-10 minutes. The car body protects you from radiant heat that would kill you outside.

techniques.name: Mobile Home Tornado Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Get out immediately

steps.instruction: Abandon the mobile home immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Mobile homes offer zero protection against tornadoes. They are often destroyed even by weak tornadoes. Do not stay.

steps.instruction: Seek a storm shelter or sturdy building nearby.
steps.helpDetail: A neighbor's house with a basement or designated community shelter is safer.

steps.instruction: If no shelter: lie flat in a low-lying ditch or depression.
steps.helpDetail: Cover your head with your hands. Protect against flying debris, which causes most injuries.

steps.instruction: Move away from the mobile home (debris hazard).
steps.helpDetail: Debris from the mobile home itself will become projectiles. Get distance.

techniques.name: Tornado Ditch Shelter
techniques.subtitle: Last resort outdoor protection

steps.instruction: Find a low spot: ditch, ravine, or depression.
steps.helpDetail: You want to be below the level of the wind-blown debris. Gravity is your shield.

steps.instruction: Lie face down, hands covering head and neck.
steps.helpDetail: Protect your skull and spine. Minimize your profile.

steps.instruction: Stay away from trees, cars, and poles.
steps.helpDetail: These can be blown onto you. Open ground is safer than near vertical hazards.

steps.instruction: Watch for flash flooding in the ditch.
steps.helpDetail: Tornadoes come with heavy rain. Be ready to move if the ditch fills rapidly with water.

techniques.name: Driving Near Tornado
techniques.subtitle: Assess path and evade

steps.instruction: Determine the tornado's path relative to you.
steps.helpDetail: If it's not moving left or right, it's moving toward you. Tornadoes usually move SW to NE.

steps.instruction: Drive at a right angle to the tornado's path.
steps.helpDetail: Do not try to outrun it straight on. Move sideways to get out of the 'bear's cage'.

steps.instruction: If imminent impact: abandon car for a ditch.
steps.helpDetail: Cars are death traps in tornadoes (tossed like toys). If you can't drive away, get low in a ditch.

steps.instruction: Do NOT hide under an overpass.
steps.helpDetail: Winds accelerate under bridges (Venturi effect). Debris is channeled there. It is a very dangerous spot.

techniques.name: Black Bear Avoidance
techniques.subtitle: Intimidate and back away

steps.instruction: Make yourself look large.
steps.helpDetail: Raise arms, open jacket. Stand on a rock. Do not crouch or hide.

steps.instruction: Make check noise, shout firmly: 'Hey bear! Go away!'
steps.helpDetail: Black bears are usually timid. Aggressive, loud human noises scare them off.

steps.instruction: Back away slowly. Do NOT run.
steps.helpDetail: Running triggers a predatory chase instinct. Back away while facing the bear.

steps.instruction: If it follows, throw rocks/sticks and get aggressive.
steps.helpDetail: Show you are not prey. You are a threat.

techniques.name: Grizzly Avoidance
techniques.subtitle: De-escalate and retreat

steps.instruction: Stop. Do not run. Do not yell.
steps.helpDetail: Grizzlies are territorial. Running triggers chase. Yelling may be seen as aggression.

steps.instruction: Speak softly and calmly: 'Hey bear, whoa bear'.
steps.helpDetail: Identify yourself as human, not prey. Keep voice low and monotonous.

steps.instruction: Avoid eye contact (seen as a challenge).
steps.helpDetail: Look at the bear's chest or feet. Turning your head slightly shows submission.

steps.instruction: Back away slowly while facing the bear.
steps.helpDetail: Move only when the bear is not advancing. Give it space.

techniques.name: Snake Avoidance
techniques.subtitle: Step back and leave it alone

steps.instruction: Freeze. Locate the snake.
steps.helpDetail: Movement may trigger a strike. Find exactly where it is.

steps.instruction: Identify safe escape route (away from head).
steps.helpDetail: Snakes can strike 1/2 their body length. Ensure your path is clear.

steps.instruction: Back away slowly and deliberately.
steps.helpDetail: Give the snake a wide berth (at least 6 feet). Most bites happen when people try to move/kill snakes.

steps.instruction: Stomp feet as you retreat (vibrations warn others).
steps.helpDetail: Snakes sense vibration. Heavy steps alert them to your presence.

techniques.name: Lightning Distance
techniques.subtitle: Flash-to-Bang Calculation

steps.instruction: See the Flash -> Start Counting Seconds.
steps.helpDetail: One-Mississippi, Two-Mississippi...

steps.instruction: Stop counting when you hear the Bang (Thunder).
steps.helpDetail: Sound travels 1 mile in 5 seconds.

steps.instruction: Divide seconds by 5 = Distance in Miles.
steps.helpDetail: 10 seconds = 2 miles. 30 seconds = 6 miles. Under 30 seconds (6 miles) is the danger zone.

steps.instruction: If under 30 seconds: Seek shelter immediately.
steps.helpDetail: You are within striking distance. 30 minutes must pass after the last thunder before leaving shelter.

techniques.name: Sinkhole Response
techniques.subtitle: React safely to sudden ground collapse

steps.instruction: If the ground starts cracking or sagging: move IMMEDIATELY away from the area.
steps.helpDetail: Sinkholes can expand rapidly. Run perpendicular to the collapse direction. Do not try to grab anything — just move.

steps.instruction: If caught in a sinkhole: try to reach the edge. Grab roots, rocks, or ledge material.
steps.helpDetail: Most sinkholes taper toward the edges. If sliding in, spread out to distribute weight and slow descent. Swim through loose material.
steps.illustrationDescription: If caught in a sinkhole: try t

steps.instruction: Alert others: yell for help. Make noise continuously so rescuers can locate you.
steps.helpDetail: Sinkholes can be deep and obscured from view. Voice is your primary locator. Whistle if available.
steps.illustrationDescription: Alert others: yell for help. M

steps.instruction: After escaping: mark the area clearly so others avoid it. Do not approach the edge — it may continue collapsing.
steps.helpDetail: Sinkholes can expand for hours or days after initial collapse. Stay at least 50 feet from edges. The surrounding ground may be undermined.

steps.instruction: Camp AWAY from sinkhole terrain: limestone, karst topography, areas with many surface depressions.
steps.helpDetail: Sinkholes occur where soluble bedrock (limestone) dissolves underground. Many small depressions in terrain suggest active subsurface erosion.

techniques.name: Mudslide Survival
techniques.subtitle: Survive and evade a mudslide or debris flow

steps.instruction: Warning signs: increased water flow in streams, cracking ground, tilting trees, unusual sounds from uphill.
steps.helpDetail: Mudslides often follow heavy rain. If streams turn muddy when it's not raining at your location, rain upstream may trigger a slide.

steps.instruction: If you hear a mudslide approaching (roaring sound uphill): run LATERALLY away from the flow path, not downhill.
steps.helpDetail: Mudslides follow valleys and drainages. Moving sideways out of the drainage takes you out of the flow path fastest.
steps.illustrationDescription: If you hear a mudslide approac

steps.instruction: If caught: curl into a tight ball, protect your head, keep mouth and nose covered.
steps.helpDetail: The debris flow will tumble you. A fetal position protects organs and head. Cover airways — mudslide suffocation is a major killer.
steps.illustrationDescription: If caught: curl into a tight b

steps.instruction: After the flow passes: extract yourself immediately. Mud creates suction — work arms free first, then legs.
steps.helpDetail: Mud sets rapidly. Move before it hardens around you. Wiggle continuously. Create air pockets around your chest by expanding ribcage.

steps.instruction: Avoid sleeping in valleys, drainage areas, or below steep slopes after heavy rain.
steps.helpDetail: Camp on ridges or benches above drainage areas. Mudslides follow predictable paths — the most dangerous areas are at the base of slopes and in channels.

techniques.name: Dust Storm Response
techniques.subtitle: Survive a sandstorm or dust storm

steps.instruction: Signs of approaching dust storm: wall of brown/orange on the horizon, sudden wind increase, static electricity.
steps.helpDetail: Haboobs and dust storms move fast — 25-40 mph. You may have only 10-15 minutes warning. Act immediately.

steps.instruction: Seek shelter: get inside a vehicle (windows up, engine off, lights on) or behind a solid structure.
steps.helpDetail: A vehicle provides excellent dust protection. Turn engine off — dust clogs air filters and destroys engines. Turn on headlights for visibility.
steps.illustrationDescription: Seek shelter: get inside a veh

steps.instruction: If caught in the open: get to the lee side of a large rock, vehicle, or any windbreak.
steps.helpDetail: Turn your back to the wind. Crouch low — wind speed is lower near the ground. Heavier particles stay low.
steps.illustrationDescription: If caught in the open: get to

steps.instruction: Cover ALL airways: wrap cloth around nose and mouth. Protect eyes with goggles or cloth.
steps.helpDetail: Breathing dust causes respiratory damage. Fine particles (PM2.5) penetrate deep into lungs. Multiple cloth layers filter more effectively.

steps.instruction: Do NOT move during the storm. Visibility can drop to zero. Wait for it to pass.
steps.helpDetail: Dust storms typically last 15-60 minutes. Moving blindly in zero visibility leads to disorientation, injury from debris, and separation from group.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do NOT move during the storm.

techniques.name: Hailstorm Shelter Response
techniques.subtitle: Protect yourself from dangerous hail

steps.instruction: Large hail (golf ball+) can cause serious injury and even death. Get under solid cover IMMEDIATELY.
steps.helpDetail: Hailstones over 1 inch diameter impact at 40+ mph. Baseball-sized hail (rarely) reaches 100+ mph. Seek hard shelter — not just trees.

steps.instruction: Best shelter: vehicle, building, cave, rock overhang. Worst: open field, under trees (branches break).
steps.helpDetail: A vehicle's roof and windshield protect from most hail. Cover your body with a backpack, sleeping pad, or any padding if caught in the open.

steps.instruction: If no shelter: lie face-down, cover head and neck with hands, pack, or any hard object.
steps.helpDetail: The head and spine are the most vulnerable. A backpack over your neck and head provides critical protection. Curl up to minimize exposed area.

steps.instruction: Protect eyes: hail shatters and fragments fly. Keep face pressed down or covered.
steps.helpDetail: Even small hail can cause eye injury from direct impact or fragmentation. Do not look up to watch the storm.

steps.instruction: After hailstorm: check for injuries — hail injuries can be severe but not immediately painful due to cold/shock.
steps.helpDetail: Bruising, lacerations, and concussions are common. Check everyone in your group. Cold hail can mask pain initially.

techniques.name: Ice Storm Power Outage
techniques.subtitle: Survive extended power loss during ice storms

steps.instruction: Conserve heat: close off unused rooms. Hang blankets over doorways and windows for insulation.
steps.helpDetail: Reducing the heated space concentrates warmth. One room heated is better than an entire house lukewarm.

steps.instruction: Layer clothing: multiple thin layers trap air and insulate better than one thick layer.
steps.helpDetail: Wool and synthetic base layers wick moisture. Add fleece mid-layer. Outer layer blocks wind. Cover extremities — hat, gloves, thick socks.

steps.instruction: Use the outdoors as a freezer: food in a cooler outside stays cold without electricity.
steps.helpDetail: During ice storms, outdoor temperatures preserve food better than a dead fridge. Move perishables outside or to an unheated garage.
steps.illustrationDescription: Use the outdoors as a freezer:

steps.instruction: NEVER use a gas stove, charcoal grill, or generator INDOORS — carbon monoxide kills silently.
steps.helpDetail: Carbon monoxide is odorless. It kills hundreds every year during power outages. Generators must be outside, 20+ feet from windows.
steps.illustrationDescription: NEVER use a gas stove, charcoa

steps.instruction: Conserve phone/device battery for emergency calls only. Keep one charged phone off as backup.
steps.helpDetail: A dead phone = no 911 access. Airplane mode extends battery dramatically. A car charger works even if the house has no power.

techniques.name: Fog Navigation
techniques.subtitle: Navigate safely in dense fog

steps.instruction: In dense fog, visibility may drop below 50 feet. STOP moving if you cannot see landmarks.
steps.helpDetail: Moving blindly in fog leads to cliff edges, river banks, and disorientation. If uncertain of terrain, stay put until fog lifts — usually by midday.
steps.illustrationDescription: In dense fog, visibility may d

steps.instruction: Use sound for navigation: streams, wind through trees, distant traffic all provide directional cues.
steps.helpDetail: Sound travels well in fog because moisture-laden air conducts sound. Slow down and listen. Cup your ears for directional hearing.

steps.instruction: Walk with one arm extended if on a trail or road to feel for obstacles.
steps.helpDetail: Fog can be so dense you cannot see your own feet. An extended arm provides 3 feet of advance warning for branches and obstacles.
steps.illustrationDescription: Walk with one arm extended if

steps.instruction: Use a compass if available — fog completely eliminates visual references.
steps.helpDetail: A compass heading is the only reliable directional tool in fog. Set a bearing before fog closes in and follow it mechanically.
steps.illustrationDescription: Use a compass if available — f

steps.instruction: Mark your trail: break branches, stack rocks, drag a stick to leave a visible line. You may need to retrace.
steps.helpDetail: In fog, it's easy to walk in circles without realizing it. Marks let you verify forward progress and retrace if you hit a dead end.

techniques.name: Extreme Altitude Survival
techniques.subtitle: Manage above 15,000 feet without acclimatization

steps.instruction: Above 15,000 feet, available oxygen drops to ~55% of sea level. Every action takes 2-3x more effort.
steps.helpDetail: Your body cannot instantly adapt. Acclimatization takes days to weeks. If you're suddenly at high altitude, reduce all activity to minimum.

steps.instruction: Recognize altitude sickness: headache, nausea, dizziness, fatigue, difficulty sleeping.
steps.helpDetail: Mild altitude sickness (AMS) is very common above 8,000 feet. It can progress to life-threatening pulmonary or cerebral edema.

steps.instruction: CRITICAL: if symptoms worsen (confusion, stumbling, coughing pink froth, severe headache): DESCEND IMMEDIATELY.
steps.helpDetail: HACE (cerebral edema) and HAPE (pulmonary edema) kill within hours if untreated. Descent of even 1,000-2,000 feet can save a life.

steps.instruction: Hydrate aggressively — you lose 2-3x more water through breathing at altitude than at sea level.
steps.helpDetail: The air is extremely dry at altitude. You lose enormous amounts of water vapor with each breath. Drink at least 4 liters per day.

steps.instruction: Eat high-carbohydrate foods — carbs require less oxygen to metabolize than protein or fat.
steps.helpDetail: At altitude, your body processes carbs more efficiently. Sugars, grains, and starches are the ideal energy source. Avoid heavy protein meals.

techniques.name: Cave Flooding Response
techniques.subtitle: Respond to rising water inside a cave

steps.instruction: If rain is falling or forecast, DO NOT enter caves. Flash floods fill caves with zero warning.
steps.helpDetail: Rain miles away can channel through underground systems in minutes. A dry cave can become a death trap in the time it takes to explore.
steps.illustrationDescription: If rain is falling or forecast

steps.instruction: If water begins rising inside a cave: EXIT IMMEDIATELY. Do not wait to see if it stops.
steps.helpDetail: Water in caves rises fast and fills passages completely. There is no high ground inside a horizontal cave. Get out NOW.

steps.instruction: If the exit is already flooded: move to the highest point inside the cave. Keep your head above water.
steps.helpDetail: Caves often have irregular ceilings with air pockets. Press your face into any upward dome and breathe the trapped air.
steps.illustrationDescription: If the exit is already flooded

steps.instruction: Mark your height: scratch a line on the wall at water level. Check every few minutes to see if water is still rising or receding.
steps.helpDetail: If the line stays underwater, water is still rising. If the line is above water, levels are dropping. This tells you whether to wait or try to exit.
steps.illustrationDescription: Mark your height: scratch a li

steps.instruction: If forced to swim out an underwater passage: hyperventilate slightly (3-4 deep breaths), dive, and swim FOR the light.
steps.helpDetail: This is a last resort. Only attempt if you know the exit passage is short. Feel along the ceiling. Upstream = toward exit in most cases.
steps.illustrationDescription: If forced to swim out an under

techniques.name: Railroad Track Following
techniques.subtitle: Use railroad tracks to navigate to civilization

steps.instruction: Railroad tracks always lead to civilization — towns, stations, or at minimum, road crossings.
steps.helpDetail: Tracks are maintained, which means maintenance crews visit regularly. Follow the tracks and you will eventually reach people.

steps.instruction: Walk BESIDE the tracks, not on them. Trains are quieter than you expect and approach fast.
steps.helpDetail: Trains traveling 60+ mph can be nearly silent until very close, especially around curves. Walk on the service road or ballast shoulder.
steps.illustrationDescription: Walk BESIDE the tracks, not on

steps.instruction: Look for mile markers, crossing signs, and signals — these indicate proximity to roads and towns.
steps.helpDetail: Mile markers count distance from a reference point. Crossing signs mean a road intersects ahead. Signals mean a junction or station is near.

steps.instruction: Railroad bridges and trestles: DO NOT cross on foot if there is no walkway. Find an alternative crossing.
steps.helpDetail: If a train comes while you're on a trestle, there is nowhere to go. Railroad bridges are extremely dangerous for pedestrians.

steps.instruction: Follow the direction with the most recent rail polishing — a shinier rail surface indicates more traffic from that direction.
steps.helpDetail: Higher-traffic direction = more likely to reach a larger town first. Check both rails — the shinier one indicates the direction of the nearest busy area.

techniques.name: Abandoned Mine Safety
techniques.subtitle: Navigate hazards of abandoned mines

steps.instruction: NEVER enter an abandoned mine unless it is your ONLY survival option. Mines kill people every year.
steps.helpDetail: Hazards include: collapse, toxic gas, vertical shafts, bad air, flooding, unstable timber supports, and disorientation in dark passages.

steps.instruction: If you must enter: check air quality. Light a match or candle at the entrance — if it dims or goes out, the air is bad.
steps.helpDetail: Low oxygen or high CO2/methane is invisible and odorless. A failing flame is your warning. If the flame goes out, DO NOT ENTER.
steps.illustrationDescription: If you must enter: check air q

steps.instruction: Stay near the entrance. Do not explore deeper. Use the mine entrance as shelter only.
steps.helpDetail: The entrance area has the best air, most structural integrity, and easiest escape. Going deeper exponentially increases all hazards.
steps.illustrationDescription: Stay near the entrance. Do not

steps.instruction: Never touch old timber supports — they may be the only thing preventing collapse.
steps.helpDetail: Rotted timbers may be load-bearing. Bumping, leaning on, or removing them can trigger a roof collapse. Walk in the center of passages.

steps.instruction: Watch for vertical shafts (winzes) in the floor — they can be hundreds of feet deep and invisible in darkness.
steps.helpDetail: Probe the floor ahead with a stick before each step. Throw a rock ahead and listen — if you don't hear it hit, there's a shaft.

techniques.name: Wildfire Smoke Protection
techniques.subtitle: Survive exposure to wildfire smoke

steps.instruction: Wildfire smoke contains fine particles (PM2.5), carbon monoxide, and volatile organic compounds.
steps.helpDetail: PM2.5 penetrates deep into lungs — it's the most dangerous component. Even healthy adults can be severely affected by heavy smoke.

steps.instruction: Reduce exposure: stay inside with windows/doors closed. Breathe through a damp cloth if outdoors.
steps.helpDetail: Wet cloth filters some particles. N95/N100 masks offer much better protection. Bandanas and surgical masks are barely effective.

steps.instruction: Stay low — smoke rises, so breathable air is closest to the ground.
steps.helpDetail: If caught in heavy smoke outdoors, crouch or crawl. The cleanest air is within 12 inches of the ground.
steps.illustrationDescription: Stay low — smoke rises, so bre

steps.instruction: If driving: turn on headlights, reduce speed, close windows, recirculate cabin air.
steps.helpDetail: Smoke reduces visibility dramaticallySwitch AC/heat to recirculate mode — this prevents drawing smoky air into the cabin.

steps.instruction: Watch for symptoms: burning eyes, coughing, chest tightness, headache, confusion.
steps.helpDetail: Confusion indicates carbon monoxide exposure — this is an emergency. Move to cleaner air immediately. Fresh air exposure reverses mild CO poisoning.

techniques.name: Rip Current Escape
techniques.subtitle: Escape a rip current pulling you out to sea

steps.instruction: DO NOT fight the current by swimming directly toward shore. You will exhaust yourself and drown.
steps.helpDetail: Rip currents are narrow (10-30 feet wide) but fast (up to 8 mph). No human can outswim them. Fighting head-on is the #1 cause of rip current drownings.
steps.illustrationDescription: DO NOT fight the current by sw

steps.instruction: Swim PARALLEL to the shore — to either side. Rip currents are narrow. You'll escape within 50-100 feet.
steps.helpDetail: Once out of the rip channel, the normal wave action will push you back toward shore. Swim parallel until you feel the pull stop.
steps.illustrationDescription: Swim PARALLEL to the shore — t

steps.instruction: If too exhausted to swim: float on your back. Rip currents do NOT pull underwater — they move you outward.
steps.helpDetail: Floating conserves energy. The current weakens 100-200 feet from shore. Once it weakens, swim diagonally toward shore.
steps.illustrationDescription: If too exhausted to swim: floa

steps.instruction: Signal for help: wave one arm while floating. Lifeguards are trained to spot this signal.
steps.helpDetail: Do not exhaust yourself waving both arms. One arm raised while floating is the recognized 'help me' signal for water rescuers.
steps.illustrationDescription: Signal for help: wave one arm

steps.instruction: Identify rip currents BEFORE entering water: look for channels of churning water, different water color, or gaps in breaking waves.
steps.helpDetail: Rip currents appear as darker, calmer channels between breaking waves. Debris or foam moving steadily seaward marks a rip channel.

techniques.name: Cold Water Immersion Survival
techniques.subtitle: Survive sudden immersion in cold water

steps.instruction: The first 1-3 minutes: GASP REFLEX. You will involuntarily gasp and hyperventilate. Focus on controlling breathing.
steps.helpDetail: Cold shock causes an uncontrollable gasp — if your head is underwater, this drowns you instantly. Get your head above water FIRST.

steps.instruction: Minutes 3-30: SWIM FAILURE. Cold numbs your muscles. Self-rescue must happen NOW before you lose strength.
steps.helpDetail: You have approximately 10 minutes of useful movement in 50°F water. Swim to safety, grab a float, or get as much of your body out of the water as possible.

steps.instruction: HELP position: if you can't get out, assume the Heat Escape Lessening Position.
steps.helpDetail: Cross arms over chest, press thighs together, draw knees up. This protects high-heat-loss areas (armpits, groin). Minimizes heat loss by up to 50%.
steps.illustrationDescription: HELP position: if you can't ge

steps.instruction: If in a group: huddle together side by side, arms around each other, children in the center.
steps.helpDetail: Group huddling shares body heat and reduces individual heat loss. The smallest body = fastest cooling. Protect children centrally.
steps.illustrationDescription: If in a group: huddle together

steps.instruction: Hypothermia sets in after 30+ minutes. You will become confused, drowsy, and stop shivering. Do NOT fall asleep.
steps.helpDetail: Paradoxically, you feel warm as core temperature drops dangerously. Loss of shivering is a late, critical sign. Fight the urge to sleep.

techniques.name: Avalanche Survival (Swimming)
techniques.subtitle: Fighting to stay on the surface of a moving snow slide

steps.instruction: As soon as the snow slab fractures above you, YELL to alert your party and drop your heavy gear.
steps.helpDetail: Ditch your backpack, ski poles, and skis immediately. Plunging weight will anchor you deeper into the slide.
steps.illustrationDescription: As soon as the snow slab fract

steps.instruction: Fight violently to stay on the surface.
steps.helpDetail: Use a vicious breaststroke or backstroke motion. Throw your arms and legs to 'swim' upward against the churning snow. You must fight being pulled under.
steps.illustrationDescription: Fight violently to stay on the

steps.instruction: Grab onto a sturdy tree or massive rock if one is in reach.
steps.helpDetail: Hold on for your life as the snow rushes past you. If you are swept away, try to angle yourself towards the edges (flanks) of the slide, where snow moves slower.
steps.illustrationDescription: Grab onto a sturdy tree or mas

steps.instruction: As the avalanche begins to slow, immediately cup both hands firmly over your mouth and nose.
steps.helpDetail: Avalanche snow instantly hardens like concrete the second it stops moving. If you do not create an air pocket in front of your face before it stops, you will suffocate instantly.
steps.illustrationDescription: As the avalanche begins to slo

steps.instruction: Once it stops, violently thrust an arm straight up.
steps.helpDetail: If your hand breaks the surface, rescuers can find you immediately. Once encased in the 'concrete', spit. Gravity pulls saliva down, telling you which way is up.

techniques.name: Tsunami Vertical Evacuation
techniques.subtitle: Surviving the lethal surge zone

steps.instruction: Recognize the signs: a massive, prolonged earthquake near the coast, or the ocean suddenly and rapidly receding, exposing the sea floor.
steps.helpDetail: Do not go out to look at the exposed fish. A catastrophic wall of water is arriving in minutes.

steps.instruction: Immediately abandon all vehicles and run on foot.
steps.helpDetail: Coastal roads will instantly gridlock. A car is a steel coffin in a tsunami. Run.

steps.instruction: Seek natural high ground above 100 feet (30 meters) elevation, as far inland as possible.
steps.helpDetail: The wave will carry massive, lethal debris—cars, buildings, and shipping containers—which act like a blender in the water.

steps.instruction: If trapped on the flats, perform Vertical Evacuation.
steps.helpDetail: Run into a structurally reinforced concrete building (like a hotel or parking garage) and climb past the 3rd floor minimum. Do not climb wooden structures; they will be swept away.
steps.illustrationDescription: If trapped on the flats, perfo

steps.instruction: Do NOT return when the water recedes.
steps.helpDetail: A tsunami is a series of waves. The second or third wave (often arriving 30 to 60 minutes later) is frequently much larger than the first. Wait for official clearance.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do NOT return when the water r

techniques.name: Lightning Position
techniques.subtitle: Minimizing strike probability in open terrain

steps.instruction: If caught in an exposed area during a thunderstorm with no shelter available, get to the lowest point possible.
steps.helpDetail: Avoid hilltops, ridgelines, open water, and isolated tall trees.
steps.illustrationDescription: If caught in an exposed area d

steps.instruction: Crouch into a ball on the balls of your feet with your heels touching.
steps.helpDetail: This minimizes ground contact area. Lightning travels through the ground; minimum contact = minimum current flow through your body.
steps.illustrationDescription: Crouch into a ball on the ball

steps.instruction: Cover your ears, tuck your head between your knees.
steps.helpDetail: The shockwave from a nearby strike can rupture eardrums.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cover your ears, tuck your hea

steps.instruction: NEVER lie flat on the ground.
steps.helpDetail: Lying flat maximizes the surface area of your body in contact with the electrified ground, massively increasing the current flowing through you.
steps.illustrationDescription: NEVER lie flat on the ground.

steps.instruction: If in a group, spread out at least 50 feet apart.
steps.helpDetail: A single strike can arc into multiple grouped people. Separation prevents a single bolt from killing everyone.
steps.illustrationDescription: If in a group, spread out at l

techniques.name: Tornado Survival (Open Field)
techniques.subtitle: Last-resort positioning when caught without shelter

steps.instruction: If you cannot reach a sturdy building or underground shelter, look for a low ditch or depression.
steps.helpDetail: Never shelter under an overpass; the wind-tunnel effect accelerates debris to lethal velocities.
steps.illustrationDescription: If you cannot reach a sturdy b

steps.instruction: Lie face down in the lowest spot you can find.
steps.helpDetail: Cover the back of your head and neck with your hands.
steps.illustrationDescription: Lie face down in the lowest sp

steps.instruction: If in a vehicle, do NOT try to outrun the tornado unless you can clearly see it moving perpendicular to you.
steps.helpDetail: Tornados can move at 70+ mph and change direction instantly.
steps.illustrationDescription: If in a vehicle, do NOT try to

steps.instruction: If driving and a tornado is approaching head-on, pull over, put the vehicle in park, keep your seatbelt on, and duck below the windows.
steps.helpDetail: Cover yourself with a blanket or jacket to protect from flying glass.
steps.illustrationDescription: If driving and a tornado is ap

steps.instruction: After the tornado passes, watch for downed power lines, gas leaks, and structural collapse.
steps.helpDetail: Do not enter damaged buildings.

techniques.name: Wildfire Escape Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Surviving entrapment in a fast-moving brush fire

steps.instruction: Move downhill and downwind toward already-burned areas (the black).
steps.helpDetail: Fire travels uphill 4x faster than on flat ground. Never run uphill away from a fire.

steps.instruction: Look for natural firebreaks: roads, rivers, rock scree fields, lakes.
steps.helpDetail: Get into water if available, but beware of superheated air above the surface.

steps.instruction: If entrapped, find a cleared or low-fuel area and lie face down.
steps.helpDetail: Scrape away all flammable material in a 10-foot radius. Remove synthetic clothing that will melt.
steps.illustrationDescription: If entrapped, find a cleared o

steps.instruction: Cover yourself with a wet blanket, dirt, or a fire shelter if available.
steps.helpDetail: Breathe through a wet cloth pressed tightly against your face to filter superheated air.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cover yourself with a wet blan

steps.instruction: Stay down until the fire front passes completely.
steps.helpDetail: The main flame front often passes in 1–3 minutes. Embers and radiant heat remain dangerous for much longer.
steps.illustrationDescription: Stay down until the fire front

techniques.name: Desert Water Rationing Strategy
techniques.subtitle: Managing hydration when water is critically limited

steps.instruction: NEVER ration water. Drink what you have when you are thirsty.
steps.helpDetail: Dying with a full canteen is one of the most common desert survival fatalities. Your body needs water NOW, not later.

steps.instruction: Ration SWEAT instead.
steps.helpDetail: Move only at night. Rest in total shade during the day. Keep clothing ON to trap sweat against your skin for evaporative cooling.

steps.instruction: Stay off the ground surface.
steps.helpDetail: Sand temperature at ground level can exceed 160°F. Elevate yourself even 18 inches on a raised platform and the temperature drops 30°F.

steps.instruction: Breathe through your nose, not your mouth.
steps.helpDetail: Nasal breathing recovers 30% more moisture than mouth breathing.

steps.instruction: Signs of severe dehydration: dizziness, confusion, cessation of sweating, dark urine.
steps.helpDetail: If sweating stops in extreme heat, you are approaching heat stroke and death. Stop all activity immediately.

techniques.name: Arctic Travel Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Moving safely through sub-zero environments

steps.instruction: Regulate your temperature to avoid sweating.
steps.helpDetail: Sweat soaking into your clothing will freeze and kill you. Open zippers and remove layers BEFORE you start sweating.

steps.instruction: Travel on frozen rivers and lakes only after testing ice.
steps.helpDetail: Minimum safe thickness: 4 inches for walking, 8 inches for a snowmobile. Jump on the ice near shore first. Clear blue ice is strongest.
steps.illustrationDescription: Travel on frozen rivers and la

steps.instruction: Navigate using the wind.
