steps.helpDetail: In polar regions, prevailing winds create sastrugi (hard snow ridges) that always point in the same direction. Use them as a constant compass.

steps.instruction: Never touch metal with bare skin.
steps.helpDetail: At extreme cold, your skin will freeze-bond to metal instantly, tearing off when you attempt to pull away.
steps.illustrationDescription: Never touch metal with bare sk

steps.instruction: Keep water bottles inside your clothing against your body.
steps.helpDetail: Water left in outer pockets will freeze solid within an hour at -20°F.

techniques.name: Life Raft Survival Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Surviving days to weeks adrift in the open ocean

steps.instruction: Immediately upon boarding the raft: administer seasickness medication if available.
steps.helpDetail: Vomiting causes catastrophic fluid loss. Seasickness is the #1 killer of raft survivors because dehydration follows vomiting.

steps.instruction: Collect rainwater using every available surface.
steps.helpDetail: Spread tarps, plastic sheeting, and clothing to catch rain. Even dew on the raft's canopy can be wrung out. Prioritize water over food.

steps.instruction: Shade yourself during the day.
steps.helpDetail: Sunburn causes fluid loss through the damaged skin. Skin cancer is irrelevant; dehydration kills in 3 days.

steps.instruction: Fish using improvised hooks, lures, or a shirt dragged behind the raft as a net.
steps.helpDetail: Fish eyes and spinal fluid contain freshwater. Do NOT eat fish if you have no water; digesting protein requires water.

steps.instruction: Signal constantly.
steps.helpDetail: Deploy sea dye at dawn and dusk when SAR flights are most likely. Flash signal mirrors at any aircraft. Activate EPIRB/PLB and leave it on.

techniques.name: River Crossing Technique
techniques.subtitle: Safely fording a fast-moving stream on foot

steps.instruction: Scout the river before crossing.
steps.helpDetail: Look for the widest section — it is usually the shallowest. Avoid narrow channels where the water is deepest and fastest.

steps.instruction: Never cross where the water is above your waist.
steps.helpDetail: At waist depth, the current can easily sweep an adult off their feet. The force of water increases exponentially with depth.
steps.illustrationDescription: Never cross where the water is

steps.instruction: Remove your pants and socks.
steps.helpDetail: Wet clothing dramatically increases drag and waterlogging weight. Keep your boots ON for traction on slippery rocks.

steps.instruction: Face upstream and cross diagonally, angling slightly downstream.
steps.helpDetail: Use a sturdy walking stick planted upstream as a third point of contact. Shuffle your feet; never cross one foot over the other.

steps.instruction: If you are swept off your feet, float on your back with feet downstream.
steps.helpDetail: Your feet act as bumpers against rocks. Do NOT try to stand until the water is shallow enough to crawl.
steps.illustrationDescription: If you are swept off your feet

techniques.name: Bear Encounter Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Surviving a close encounter with black or grizzly bears

steps.instruction: Identify the species. Black bears are smaller with straight faces and tall ears. Grizzlies have a distinctive shoulder hump and dish-shaped face.
steps.helpDetail: Your response depends entirely on the species.

steps.instruction: For both: DO NOT RUN.
steps.helpDetail: Bears can sprint at 35 mph. Running triggers their predatory chase instinct. You cannot outrun a bear.

steps.instruction: GRIZZLY (defensive encounter): Play dead.
steps.helpDetail: Drop to the ground face down, spread your legs to resist being rolled, cover the back of your neck with your hands. Stay silent. The bear is defending territory; once it perceives you as non-threatening, it will leave.

steps.instruction: BLACK BEAR: Fight back aggressively.
steps.helpDetail: Black bears are more timid but sometimes predatory. Punch, kick, scream, and use rocks or sticks. Do NOT play dead with a black bear — it will eat you.

steps.instruction: Deploy bear spray at 20 feet if available.
steps.helpDetail: Bear spray is statistically more effective than firearms at stopping a charge. Aim for the face in a wide fog pattern.
steps.illustrationDescription: Deploy bear spray at 20 feet i

techniques.name: Snake Encounter Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Avoiding snakebite and managing envenomation

steps.instruction: Most snakebites occur when people try to catch or kill snakes.
steps.helpDetail: Leave it alone. Back away slowly. Most snakes will flee if given space.

steps.instruction: Wear boots and long pants in snake country.
steps.helpDetail: 80% of bites are on the feet and lower legs. Thick leather boots stop most fangs.
steps.illustrationDescription: Wear boots and long pants in s

steps.instruction: If bitten: remove rings, watches, and tight clothing near the bite.
steps.helpDetail: Swelling will be severe and rapid. Constriction from jewelry cuts off circulation.

steps.instruction: Do NOT: cut the bite, suck out venom, apply ice, or apply a tourniquet.
steps.helpDetail: These Hollywood myths cause more tissue damage than the venom itself.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do NOT: cut the bite, suck out

steps.instruction: Immobilize the bitten limb below heart level, keep the patient calm, and evacuate.
steps.helpDetail: The patient's heart rate directly determines how fast venom circulates. Panic kills faster than venom.

techniques.name: Hypothermia Stage Assessment
techniques.subtitle: Identifying mild, moderate, and severe hypothermia progression

steps.instruction: MILD (95–90°F / 35–32°C): Shivering, goosebumps, fumbling hands, stumbling.
steps.helpDetail: The patient is still alert and can help themselves. Rewarm aggressively: get them dry, add layers, give warm drinks.

steps.instruction: MODERATE (90–82°F / 32–28°C): Violent shivering or shivering STOPS. Confusion, slurred speech, poor decisions.
steps.helpDetail: They may resist help or claim they are fine. They are not. They can no longer rewarm themselves.

steps.instruction: SEVERE (<82°F / <28°C): No shivering. Rigid muscles. Unconscious or semi-conscious. Paradoxical undressing.
steps.helpDetail: The patient may start removing clothing due to a final burst of warm blood from the core to the skin.

steps.instruction: Handle severe hypothermia patients EXTREMELY gently.
steps.helpDetail: Rough handling can cause the cold, acidotic blood in the extremities to rush into the heart, causing lethal cardiac arrhythmia (afterdrop).

steps.instruction: Rewarm the CORE, not the extremities.
steps.helpDetail: Apply heat to the chest, neck, armpits, and groin. Do NOT rub the extremities or put them in warm water. Evacuate immediately.
steps.illustrationDescription: Rewarm the CORE, not the extre

techniques.name: Heat Exhaustion vs Heat Stroke
techniques.subtitle: Differentiating the two stages before it becomes fatal

steps.instruction: HEAT EXHAUSTION: Heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, headache, dizziness, normal or slightly elevated temperature.
steps.helpDetail: The body is still attempting to cool itself. This is the WARNING stage.

steps.instruction: Treatment for heat exhaustion: Move to shade, remove excess clothing, drink cool water, apply cool wet cloths.
steps.helpDetail: Rest for at least 1 hour before resuming activity.

steps.instruction: HEAT STROKE: Sweating STOPS. Hot, dry, red skin. Confusion or unconsciousness. Core temp above 104°F.
steps.helpDetail: This is a LETHAL EMERGENCY. The thermoregulation system has failed.

steps.instruction: Heat stroke treatment: Aggressive emergency cooling.
steps.helpDetail: Douse with cold water, fan vigorously, pack ice at neck/armpits/groin. Every minute of delay causes brain damage.

steps.instruction: The transition from heat exhaustion to heat stroke can happen in under 30 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: If a heat exhaustion patient stops sweating and becomes confused, treat as heat stroke immediately.

techniques.name: Altitude Acclimatization Schedule
techniques.subtitle: Preventing altitude illness through controlled ascent

steps.instruction: Above 8,000 feet (2,400m), increase sleeping altitude by no more than 1,000 feet (300m) per day.
steps.helpDetail: You can climb higher during the day, but sleep low. 'Climb high, sleep low.'

steps.instruction: For every 3,000 feet gained, take a rest day.
steps.helpDetail: Do not simply push through symptoms. Your body needs time to produce additional red blood cells.

steps.instruction: Hydrate aggressively: 3–4 liters of water per day at altitude.
steps.helpDetail: Altitude causes massive insensible water loss through rapid, dry breathing.

steps.instruction: Avoid alcohol and sleeping pills.
steps.helpDetail: Both suppress respiration, which is already compromised at altitude. Alcohol also dehydrates.

steps.instruction: Prophylactic Acetazolamide (Diamox) accelerates acclimatization.
steps.helpDetail: Take 125mg twice daily starting 24 hours before ascent. Side effects: tingling fingers and frequent urination.

techniques.name: Night Movement Evasion
techniques.subtitle: Moving undetected through hostile terrain at night

steps.instruction: Move only during the darkest 4 hours (typically 0100-0500).
steps.helpDetail: Your eyes need 30 minutes to fully dark-adapt. Avoid looking at any light source.

steps.instruction: Use the fox walk: Place the outside edge of your foot down first, roll inward, test for noise.
steps.helpDetail: Normal heel-strike walking snaps twigs. The fox walk is nearly silent on forest floor.
steps.illustrationDescription: Use the fox walk: Place the ou

steps.instruction: Navigate by stars or compass with luminous dial.
steps.helpDetail: Never use a flashlight or phone screen. A single flash is visible for 2+ miles.
steps.illustrationDescription: Navigate by stars or compass w

steps.instruction: Stay in shadows and avoid silhouetting against the sky on ridgelines.
steps.helpDetail: Crouch below ridgelines and move along the military crest (2/3 up the slope).
steps.illustrationDescription: Stay in shadows and avoid silh

steps.instruction: If you hear pursuers, freeze completely and remain motionless.
steps.helpDetail: Motion triggers peripheral vision detection far more than shape or color.
steps.illustrationDescription: If you hear pursuers, freeze c

techniques.name: Anti-Tracking Techniques
techniques.subtitle: Confusing human or dog trackers following your trail

steps.instruction: Walk in streams for at least 100 meters before exiting on rocky ground.
steps.helpDetail: Water eliminates foot scent and destroys visible tracks.
steps.illustrationDescription: Walk in streams for at least 1

steps.instruction: Walk backward for 50 meters, then sidestep to a hard surface.
steps.helpDetail: This creates a false trail showing you returning the way you came.

steps.instruction: Use the buttonhook: walk past your intended turn, continue 100m, then circle back at a right angle.
steps.helpDetail: Trackers follow momentum. The buttonhook breaks their predictive model.

steps.instruction: Cross hard surfaces (rock, pavement, gravel) whenever possible.
steps.helpDetail: No track impression forms on hard surfaces.

steps.instruction: If dogs are tracking, cross through areas with strong-smelling plants (wild onion, camphor).
steps.helpDetail: Strong odors overwhelm the dog's scent picture.
steps.illustrationDescription: If dogs are tracking, cross th

techniques.name: Camouflage Face Paint Application
techniques.subtitle: Breaking up the human face outline with natural materials

steps.instruction: The human face is highly recognizable due to its symmetry and light-dark contrast.
steps.helpDetail: Camouflage breaks this pattern.
steps.illustrationDescription: The human face is highly recog

steps.instruction: Darken light areas (forehead, nose, cheekbones, chin) with mud, charcoal, or dark clay.
steps.helpDetail: These naturally catch light and create shine.

steps.instruction: Lighten dark areas (eye sockets, under nose, neck shadows) with lighter clay or ash.
steps.helpDetail: This eliminates the familiar face-shadow pattern.

steps.instruction: Use irregular, diagonal stripes — never horizontal or symmetrical patterns.
steps.helpDetail: The human brain is wired to detect symmetry. Asymmetry defeats recognition.

steps.instruction: Cover all exposed skin including ears, backs of hands, and neck.
steps.helpDetail: A camouflaged face with visible white hands is useless.

techniques.name: Dog Team Evasion
techniques.subtitle: Specific techniques to defeat tracking dogs

steps.instruction: Move downwind of your trail whenever possible.
steps.helpDetail: Dogs track airborne scent cones. Downwind movement forces the handler to loop upwind to re-acquire.
steps.illustrationDescription: Move downwind of your trail wh

steps.instruction: Wade through water crossings at least 200 meters long.
steps.helpDetail: Short water crossings do not eliminate the scent cone, just interrupt the ground trail.

steps.instruction: Move through areas with strong competing scents: farms, garbage, wild garlic patches.
steps.helpDetail: Dogs can still differentiate, but it slows them significantly.

steps.instruction: Pepper spray or chili powder on your trail causes temporary nasal pain and disruption.
steps.helpDetail: This is a last resort and may not stop well-trained military tracking dogs.
steps.illustrationDescription: Pepper spray or chili powder o

steps.instruction: The most effective dog evasion is SPEED and DISTANCE.
steps.helpDetail: Dogs tire. Handlers tire faster. Putting 5+ miles between you and the last contact point takes you out of the scent cone.

techniques.name: Checkpoint Bypass
techniques.subtitle: Avoiding or navigating through controlled access points

steps.instruction: Observe the checkpoint from concealment for at least 30 minutes before attempting bypass.
steps.helpDetail: Note guard rotation, blind spots, vehicle patterns, and lighting.

steps.instruction: Bypass by routing around it through terrain that is too difficult for guards to watch.
steps.helpDetail: Checkpoints are usually on roads. Off-road terrain is rarely patrolled.
steps.illustrationDescription: Bypass by routing around it th

steps.instruction: Move during shift changes or high-traffic periods when guards are distracted.
steps.helpDetail: The transition between guard shifts creates 5-10 minutes of reduced alertness.
steps.illustrationDescription: Move during shift changes or h

steps.instruction: If you must pass through, blend with local pedestrian or vehicle traffic.
steps.helpDetail: Match clothing, posture, and pace of local people perfectly.
steps.illustrationDescription: If you must pass through, blen

steps.instruction: Have a cover story with verifiable details prepared.
steps.helpDetail: A story with specific, mundane details (visiting a relative, going to market) is more convincing than a vague one.
steps.illustrationDescription: Have a cover story with verifi

techniques.name: Shark Deterrent Techniques
techniques.subtitle: Reducing shark encounter risk while adrift or swimming

steps.instruction: Do not urinate or defecate in the water if sharks are present.
steps.helpDetail: Body waste creates chemical signals that attract sharks.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do not urinate or defecate in

steps.instruction: Do not trail hands or feet over the edge of a raft.
steps.helpDetail: Dangling limbs look like prey from below.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do not trail hands or feet ove

steps.instruction: If a shark bumps or circles you, face it and maintain eye contact.
steps.helpDetail: Strike the shark firmly on the nose, eyes, or gill slits if it comes within arm's reach.
steps.illustrationDescription: If a shark bumps or circles yo

steps.instruction: Deploy sea dye marker if available.
steps.helpDetail: The dark cloud created by dye obscures you from the shark's vision.
steps.illustrationDescription: Deploy sea dye marker if avail

steps.instruction: Avoid splashing or erratic movements.
steps.helpDetail: Irregular vibrations mimic injured prey and trigger attack behavior.

techniques.name: Seasickness Acupressure
techniques.subtitle: Using pressure point P6 to reduce nausea without medication

steps.instruction: Locate the Nei-Kuan point (P6): 3 finger-widths below the wrist crease, between the two central tendons.
steps.helpDetail: Press firmly with your thumb.

steps.instruction: Apply steady, firm pressure for 2-3 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: The mechanism is believed to interrupt nausea signals traveling the vagus nerve.

steps.instruction: Commercial Sea-Bands use this same principle with a plastic bead.
steps.helpDetail: Wearing bands on both wrists provides continuous stimulation.

steps.instruction: Fix your gaze on the horizon.
steps.helpDetail: This resolves the sensory mismatch between your inner ear (detecting motion) and your eyes.
steps.illustrationDescription: Fix your gaze on the horizon.

steps.instruction: If vomiting occurs, do so over the DOWNWIND side of the raft.
steps.helpDetail: Vomit trailing in the water attracts sharks and wastes precious body fluid.
steps.illustrationDescription: If vomiting occurs, do so over

techniques.name: Life Raft Repair
techniques.subtitle: Patching punctures and air leaks in inflatable rafts

steps.instruction: Locate the leak by listening for hissing or smearing soapy water (if available) over the surface.
steps.helpDetail: Bubbles form at the puncture site.
steps.illustrationDescription: Locate the leak by listening f

steps.instruction: Use the repair kit included in the raft's survival kit.
steps.helpDetail: Most kits include rubber patches, adhesive, and a roller.
steps.illustrationDescription: Use the repair kit included in

steps.instruction: If no repair kit: chewing gum, duct tape, or melted rubber from shoes can serve as improvised sealant.
steps.helpDetail: Press hard and hold for 5+ minutes.
steps.illustrationDescription: If no repair kit: chewing gum,

steps.instruction: Keep the raft fully inflated by topping off with the hand pump every 2-4 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Air contracts in cold nighttime temperatures and expands in daytime heat.
steps.illustrationDescription: Keep the raft fully inflated b

steps.instruction: If a major chamber fails, redistribute weight to the intact chambers.
steps.helpDetail: Most rafts have multiple independent air chambers for exactly this reason.
steps.illustrationDescription: If a major chamber fails, redi

techniques.name: Raft Water Discipline Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Rationing freshwater to maximize survival time at sea

steps.instruction: Do NOT drink any water for the first 24 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Your body has reserves. This initial fast primes your kidneys to conserve water.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do NOT drink any water for the

steps.instruction: After 24 hours, ration water to 500ml (1 pint) per day per person.
steps.helpDetail: Sip small amounts throughout the day rather than drinking all at once.

steps.instruction: NEVER drink seawater.
steps.helpDetail: The 3.5% salt concentration forces your kidneys to use more water to flush the salt than you ingested. It accelerates death.

steps.instruction: Collect every drop of rain using the raft canopy, tarps, and clothing.
steps.helpDetail: Pre-rinse collection surfaces with the first rainfall to wash off salt deposits.
steps.illustrationDescription: Collect every drop of rain usi

steps.instruction: Reduce water loss: stay in shade, minimize movement, keep skin covered.
steps.helpDetail: Talking and breathing through the mouth wastes moisture. Breathe through the nose.
steps.illustrationDescription: Reduce water loss: stay in sha

techniques.name: Edible Barnacle Harvesting
techniques.subtitle: Scraping and eating barnacles from raft bottoms

steps.instruction: Goose barnacles attach to the underside of rafts and floating debris within days of deployment.
steps.helpDetail: They are edible and provide protein and moisture.

steps.instruction: Scrape them off carefully with a knife or sharp edge.
steps.helpDetail: Watch for sharp shells that can puncture the raft.

steps.instruction: Crack open the shell and eat the stalk (neck) portion raw.
steps.helpDetail: The texture is similar to crab meat. The taste is mildly oceanic.
steps.illustrationDescription: Crack open the shell and eat t

steps.instruction: Small crabs and fish often hide among barnacle clusters.
steps.helpDetail: Check for additional protein sources clinging to the raft bottom.
steps.illustrationDescription: Small crabs and fish often hid

steps.instruction: Do not eat barnacles if you have no freshwater.
steps.helpDetail: Digesting protein requires water. Eating protein without water accelerates dehydration.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do not eat barnacles if you ha

techniques.name: Ice Fishing Through Jigging
techniques.subtitle: Catching fish through a hole in frozen lakes

steps.instruction: Verify ice thickness: minimum 4 inches of clear blue ice for walking.
steps.helpDetail: Bore a test hole near shore first. White or snow ice is weaker than clear ice.

steps.instruction: Chop or drill a hole 8-10 inches in diameter through the ice.
steps.helpDetail: An axe, ice chisel, or improvised T-handle drill works.
steps.illustrationDescription: Chop or drill a hole 8-10 inch

steps.instruction: Scoop out ice chips from the hole.
steps.helpDetail: The clearer the hole, the better you can see fish approaching.
steps.illustrationDescription: Scoop out ice chips from the h

steps.instruction: Drop a baited hook or bright lure to the bottom, then lift it 6 inches off the bottom.
steps.helpDetail: Jig (bounce) the lure rhythmically. The motion attracts fish in the cold, low-visibility water.
steps.illustrationDescription: Drop a baited hook or bright l

steps.instruction: Fish caught through ice keep indefinitely in sub-freezing conditions.
steps.helpDetail: Simply lay them on the ice surface. Natural freezing preserves them until you are ready to cook.
steps.illustrationDescription: Fish caught through ice keep i

techniques.name: Vapor Barrier Liner System
techniques.subtitle: Preventing moisture from entering insulation in extreme cold

steps.instruction: Below 0°F, body moisture migrating outward through insulation layers freezes inside the insulation.
steps.helpDetail: This ice buildup destroys insulation value over days, eventually making your sleeping bag useless.

steps.instruction: Wear a vapor barrier (plastic bag, rain jacket) NEXT TO YOUR SKIN, inside all insulation.
steps.helpDetail: This feels clammy initially but prevents moisture from entering the insulation.

steps.instruction: Your skin quickly reaches 100% humidity and sweating stops.
steps.helpDetail: The body's thermoregulation adjusts within 20-30 minutes. You feel normal.

steps.instruction: Apply the same principle to feet: wear a plastic bag between your sock liner and outer sock.
steps.helpDetail: This keeps your boot insulation dry, dramatically extending its effective life.
steps.illustrationDescription: Apply the same principle to fe

steps.instruction: At the end of each day, turn your sleeping bag inside out and beat the frost crystals off.
steps.helpDetail: This recovers any lost loft. Combine with vapor barrier liners for maximum insulation longevity.
steps.illustrationDescription: At the end of each day, turn y

techniques.name: Landslide (Indoors)
techniques.subtitle: Survive a debris flow inside a structure

steps.instruction: Do NOT attempt to flee the house if the slide is already occurring.
steps.helpDetail: Running outside during an active debris flow exposes you to massive kinetic force. Structure walls provide a physical barrier.

steps.instruction: Move to a second story or the roof immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Debris flows quickly fill ground-level rooms with mud, rocks, and trees. Vertical distance is your only leverage.

steps.instruction: If trapped on the ground floor, curl into a tight ball under sturdy furniture.
steps.helpDetail: A heavy desk or table can create a void space if the ceiling collapses or debris breaches the walls. Protect your head and neck.

steps.instruction: Cover your mouth and nose with cloth.
steps.helpDetail: Mudslide fatalities are frequently caused by suffocation, not blunt trauma. Siftable dust and mud will flood the air space.

techniques.name: Landslide (Vehicle)
techniques.subtitle: Respond to a mudslide while driving

steps.instruction: If you see a slide approaching ahead, stop and reverse immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Do not attempt to 'punch through' a small slide on the road. The main debris flow is likely trailing closely behind the initial slurry.

steps.instruction: If debris blocks your path, ABANDON THE VEHICLE instantly.
steps.helpDetail: Cars are death traps in landslides. They will be crushed or buried in seconds. A vehicle provides zero structural protection against tons of earth.

steps.instruction: Run strictly laterally (sideways), out of the flow path.
steps.helpDetail: Never run downhill away from a slide. Mud flows move at 35+ mph. Your only chance is moving horizontally up an embankment to get out of the channel.

techniques.name: Landslide (Outdoors)
techniques.subtitle: Evasive action for ground-level debris flows

steps.instruction: Listen for roaring, cracking trees, or rushing water.
steps.helpDetail: If streams turn inexplicably muddy or you hear a freight-train sound uphill, a slide is imminent. Do not wait to see it.

steps.instruction: Run perpendicular to the slope, moving laterally out of the basin.
steps.helpDetail: Debris naturally channels into ravines, valleys, and creek beds. You must gain lateral distance out of the immediate drainage corridor. Never run straight downhill.

steps.instruction: If escape is impossible, curl tightly into a fetal position.
steps.helpDetail: Protect your head and vital organs. Debris will tumble you. Try to position yourself behind a massive, anchored object (like a giant boulder), but avoid trees which will snap.

techniques.name: Swiftwater Survival Swimming
techniques.subtitle: Defensive posture in fast currents

steps.instruction: Assume the defensive swimming position: on your back, feet downstream, head upstream.
steps.helpDetail: Keep your toes visible at the surface. Fend off rocks and debris with your feet. Keep your head high to breathe.

steps.instruction: NEVER try to stand up in moving water deeper than your knees.
steps.helpDetail: Foot entrapment is deadly. If your foot gets wedged in rocks and the current pushes you over, the force of the water will hold you under and drown you. Swim out, don't walk.

steps.instruction: Use the ferry angle to move laterally to shore.
steps.helpDetail: In the defensive position, point your head toward the shore you want at a 45-degree angle. Backpaddle with your arms. The current will naturally ferry you toward that bank.

techniques.name: Extreme Flood: Rooftop Escape
techniques.subtitle: Survive rapidly rising indoor water

steps.instruction: Move to the highest floor immediately. Take an axe or heavy tool.
steps.helpDetail: Water can rise feet in minutes. Do not wait for rescue on the ground floor.

steps.instruction: NEVER enter a closed attic space without an axe or saw.
steps.helpDetail: If water continues to rise, a closed attic becomes a death trap. You will drown against the roof. You MUST have a way to chop through the roof from the inside.

steps.instruction: If trapped in the attic, chop a hole at the highest point securely.
steps.helpDetail: Avoid hacking directly over joists. Once through, climb onto the roof. Signal with high-contrast colors.

techniques.name: Navigating Flood Electrocution Hazards
techniques.subtitle: Detect and evade charged water

steps.instruction: Assume all standing water is electrically charged.
steps.helpDetail: Downed power lines, submerged electrical boxes, and flooded basements can electrify water invisibly. There is no bubbling or warning.

steps.instruction: Do not enter a flooded basement if the power is still on.
steps.helpDetail: Even if water is an inch deep, if it touches the electrical panel or low outlets, stepping into it is fatal. Turn off power from a dry exterior breaker if possible.

steps.instruction: If you MUST walk through suspect water, use the 'shuffle step'.
steps.helpDetail: Do not take normal strides. Keep your feet touching each other and slide them across the ground. A wide stride creates a voltage differential between your feet, causing current to flow up one leg and down the other, crossing your heart.

techniques.name: Post-Flood: Waterborne Disease Prevention
techniques.subtitle: Treat contaminated floodwater exposure

steps.instruction: Treat all floodwater as heavily contaminated raw sewage.
steps.helpDetail: Floodwater contains animal feces, agricultural chemicals, and overflowing sewage systems. Pathogen load is extreme.

steps.instruction: Clean all open wounds immediately with clean water and soap/iodine.
steps.helpDetail: Even minor scrapes exposed to floodwater can lead to severe infections, including Vibrio and Tetanus. Monitor for redness and swelling.

steps.instruction: Do NOT consume food or drink that touched floodwater.
steps.helpDetail: Even sealed plastic bottles and jars should be considered contaminated on the outside. Discard anything without a fully hermetic metal seal (and even then, boil the can before opening).

techniques.name: Trapped by Debris / Building Collapse
techniques.subtitle: Maximize survival odds inside a void

steps.instruction: Do NOT panic or move unnecessarily. Conserve oxygen.
steps.helpDetail: Dust will settle. Breathing rapidly depletes the limited oxygen in your void space and increases carbon dioxide buildup.

steps.instruction: Cover your mouth and nose with your shirt.
steps.helpDetail: Inhalation of pulverized concrete, mud, and dust is a primary cause of death in collapses. Filter the air.

steps.instruction: Tap on a pipe or wall in groups of 3.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT shout unless you hear rescuers nearby. Shouting breathes in dust and exhausts you. Tap rhythmically with a hard object (3 loud taps, pause, repeat) so rescue dogs and listening devices can detect the artificial pattern.

techniques.name: Rockfall Evasion
techniques.subtitle: Survive sudden rock descents on steep terrain

steps.instruction: Look UP instantly and identify the fall line.
steps.helpDetail: Do not run blindly. Locate the falling rocks and their trajectory. Rocks bounce unpredictably but follow the path of least resistance.

steps.instruction: Dive behind a solid barrier or large tree uphill of you.
steps.helpDetail: A massive, anchored boulder provides a deflection shield. If on an open scree slope, move laterally out of the fall line immediately.

steps.instruction: If caught without cover, minimize your profile.
steps.helpDetail: Curl into a tight ball, facing downhill, with your backpack facing uphill to absorb impact. Cover your head and neck with your arms.

techniques.sourceName: Mountaineering: The Freedom of the Hills

techniques.name: Sinkhole Escape & Evasion
techniques.subtitle: Survive sudden ground subsidence

steps.instruction: If the ground gives way, spread your arms wide immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Increasing your surface area may allow you to catch the lip of the hole instead of plumenting into the void. 'Starfish' your body.

steps.instruction: Do NOT stand near the edge. The lip will continue to collapse.
steps.helpDetail: If you are outside the hole, back away slowly while staying low. The ground bordering a sinkhole is structurally compromised and will calve off.

steps.instruction: If trapped inside, climb only on stable, embedded roots or solid rock.
steps.helpDetail: Do not pull on loose dirt or hanging debris; it will trigger secondary collapses and bury you. Wait for rope rescue if the walls are sheer dirt.

techniques.name: Sinking Vehicle Escape
techniques.subtitle: Escape a car trapped in floodwater
techniques.description: A vehicle will float for 30-60 seconds before sinking. Once submerged, water pressure makes doors impossible to open. You must exit immediately through the windows.

steps.instruction: Unbuckle seatbelts immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Do not panic. Keep your hands on the steering wheel until the car hits the water. Unbuckle your seatbelt first, then help children unbuckle.

steps.instruction: Roll down the windows before the power fails.
steps.helpDetail: Power windows usually function for 1-3 minutes underwater. If they fail, you must break the side window. NEVER break the windshield (it is designed not to shatter).

steps.instruction: Break the side window if it won't roll down.
steps.helpDetail: Use an escape tool, the metal prongs of a removable headrest, or a heavy object to strike the LOWER CORNER of the side window where the glass is weakest.

steps.instruction: Exit through the window and swim to safety.
steps.helpDetail: Push children out first. Do not grab onto the vehicle; swim with the current toward high ground or a sturdy object.

techniques.name: Emergency Sandbagging
techniques.subtitle: Create an effective flood barrier
techniques.description: Constructing a proper sandbag wall can divert low-level floodwater away from critical infrastructure. Speed and structural integrity are critical.

steps.instruction: Fill bags only half to two-thirds full.
steps.helpDetail: Do not tie the bags. Overfilled bags leave gaps and do not mold together to form a tight seal.

steps.instruction: Lay bags flat like brickwork.
steps.helpDetail: Tuck the unfilled flap under the bag across the direction of water flow. Stagger the joints in alternating layers like laying bricks.

steps.instruction: Compact each bag into place.
steps.helpDetail: Walk on the bags to pack them tightly together against the ground and adjacent bags, eliminating water channels.

steps.instruction: Build a pyramid shape if stacking high.
steps.helpDetail: The base must be three times wider than the height. A 2-foot-high wall needs a 6-foot-wide base to withstand water pressure.

techniques.name: Post-Flood Water Safety
techniques.subtitle: Survive massive water contamination
techniques.description: Floodwater is highly toxic and contaminated with sewage, chemicals, and deceased animals. Surviving the flood means surviving the waterborne diseases that follow.

steps.instruction: Assume all tap water is contaminated.
steps.helpDetail: Do not drink, brush teeth, or wash dishes with tap water until emergency services issue an 'all clear'. Floodwater overwhelms municipal treatment plants.

steps.instruction: Sanitize all wounds instantly.
steps.helpDetail: Even a minor scratch exposed to floodwater can lead to rapid sepsis or Vibrio vulnificus. Clean aggressively with bottled water and iodine.

steps.instruction: Boil water dynamically.
steps.helpDetail: Filtering is not enough for viruses. You must achieve a rolling boil for 1 full minute (3 minutes at high altitude) to kill pathogens.

techniques.name: Landslide Diagnostics
techniques.subtitle: Recognize the invisible signs of a slide
techniques.description: Landslides give subtle acoustic and structural warnings days or minutes before releasing. Identifying these signs allows for pre-emptive evacuation.

steps.instruction: Listen for low-frequency rumbling.
steps.helpDetail: A faint rumble that increases in volume is the sound of boulders knocking together underground. Trees cracking or snapping indicates earth movement.

steps.instruction: Look for new cracks in structures.
steps.helpDetail: Doors or windows sticking for the first time, new cracks in foundation, retaining walls leaning, or cracked pavement indicate the ground underneath has slipped.

steps.instruction: Observe water flow deviations.
steps.helpDetail: If a stream suddenly becomes muddy, or if water volume decreases rapidly despite rain, a debris dam has formed upstream and a massive surge is imminent.

techniques.name: Debris Flow Evacuation
techniques.subtitle: Survive a fast-moving mudslide
techniques.description: Debris flows contain boulders and trees moving at over 35 mph. You cannot outrun them linearly. You must execute lateral evasion.

steps.instruction: Do not run down the mountain.
steps.helpDetail: Debris flows travel perfectly down paths of least resistance (gullies, roads, valleys). Run PERPENDICULAR to the flow direction to escape its path.

steps.instruction: Seek immediate massive structure.
steps.helpDetail: If escape is impossible, get behind the strongest barrier available (large boulders, massive concrete bridge supports, the uphill side of large trees).

steps.instruction: Curl into the fetal position and cover your head.
steps.helpDetail: If swept up, protect your head. The primary cause of fatality in debris flows is traumatic blunt force impact from entrained debris.

techniques.name: Flood: Structural Diagnostics
techniques.subtitle: Assess building collapse risk during inundation
techniques.description: Moving water exerts immense lateral pressure. Analyzing structural deflection and foundational undercutting can prevent catastrophic collapse entrapment.

steps.instruction: Check for hydrostatic bowing.
steps.helpDetail: If basement walls are bowing inward or horizontal cracks appear in concrete blocks, hydrostatic pressure is critical. Immediately evacuate upper floors as foundation collapse is imminent.

steps.instruction: Listen for structural shearing.
steps.helpDetail: Loud popping, nail screeching, or wood splintering sounds indicate the house is shifting off its foundation. The structure is no longer safe.

steps.instruction: Equalize water pressure (Extreme Measure).
steps.helpDetail: If water is rushing against the house exterior and building up, opening basement windows to intentionally flood the basement equalizes pressure and can prevent immediate wall shear.

techniques.name: Flood: Hydrology & Current Evasion
techniques.subtitle: Read water flow to avoid sweepers and strainers
techniques.description: Urban floodwater acts like a Class IV rapid. Understanding hydrology--specifically strainers, eddies, and hydraulics--is the only way to swim to safety.

steps.instruction: Identify and avoid 'Strainers'.
steps.helpDetail: Strainers are obstacles (fences, downed trees, grates) that let water through but trap solid objects. Water pressure will pin you against them until you drown. Swim aggressively away from them.

steps.instruction: Target 'Eddies' for refuge.
steps.helpDetail: An eddy is calm water behind a solid obstruction (like a large boulder or concrete pillar) where the water flows upstream. Aim for the V-shaped calm behind barriers to rest.

steps.instruction: Escape 'Hydraulic jumps' (Keepers).
steps.helpDetail: Water flowing over a submerged barrier curves back on itself, creating a washing machine effect that traps swimmers. If caught, swim out the bottom or side—do not fight to surface in the boil.

techniques.name: Flood: Pathogen & HAZMAT Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Survive the toxic slurry of urban flooding
techniques.description: Urban floodwater mixes submerged sewage plants, chemical storage, and gasoline. Immediate chemical triage is necessary upon exiting the water.

steps.instruction: Assume Class 3 Biohazard status.
steps.helpDetail: Do not touch your face, eyes, or open wounds. Ingesting even milliliters of floodwater can cause severe dysentery, E. coli, or cholera.

steps.instruction: Strip and isolate clothing.
steps.helpDetail: Remove wet clothing immediately. Do not ring it out indoors (aerosolizes pathogens). Seal in garbage bags. The fabric is saturated with heavy metals and sewage.

steps.instruction: Sanitize with extreme dilution.
steps.helpDetail: A solution of 1/4 cup liquid bleach per gallon of water is required to kill norovirus and vibrio. Scrub skin aggressively; prioritize open lacerations.

techniques.name: Landslide: Geotech Diagnostics
techniques.subtitle: Identify earth failure tension cracks
techniques.description: Hillsides do not fail instantly; they fail sequentially. Recognizing the geotechnical phases of earth flow allows precise evacuation timing.

steps.instruction: Look for tension cracks on slopes.
steps.helpDetail: Transverse cracks extending horizontally across a slope or paved road are the primary indicator of imminent translational sliding. The earth is stretching before failure.

steps.instruction: Monitor spring water clarity.
steps.helpDetail: If a previously clear spring or seep suddenly turns muddy, the water table underground is shearing soil. The slope is lubricating for a massive slip.

steps.instruction: Observe 'J-root' tree lean.
steps.helpDetail: Trees naturally correct vertical growth. If a group of trees forms a curved 'pistol butt' trunk at the base, the soil is actively creeping downhill.

techniques.name: Landslide: Void Space Survival
techniques.subtitle: Maximize air when buried by debris
techniques.description: If trapped inside a collapsed structure or vehicle under a landslide, asphyxiation from fine clay dust is the primary killer. You must manage your air pocket.

steps.instruction: Create an immediate dust filter.
steps.helpDetail: Fine silt from mudslides will rapidly fill your lungs. Pull your shirt over your nose and mouth immediately. Breathe shallowly to avoid sucking in dust.

steps.instruction: Establish a structural void.
steps.helpDetail: If trapped under debris, move near a sturdy load-bearing wall, desk, or car frame. Do not push against the mud itself; it acts as a fluid and will collapse inward.

steps.instruction: Conserve oxygen & signal rhythmically.
steps.helpDetail: Do not yell continuously—mud absorbs sound and you waste oxygen. Tap out groups of three rhythms on a metal pipe or hard surface using a rock or tool.

steps.instruction: Identify spatial orientation.
steps.helpDetail: If completely buried and disoriented in the dark, spit out a small amount of saliva. Gravity will pull it down, telling you which way is "up" if digging becomes possible.

techniques.name: Swiftwater Survival Position
techniques.subtitle: Surviving river rapids

steps.instruction: Do not attempt to stand up.
steps.helpDetail: Putting your feet down in fast-moving water over knee-deep can lead to a fatal foot entrapment.

steps.instruction: Roll onto your back.
steps.helpDetail: Float on your back, keeping your head elevated.

steps.instruction: Point feet downstream.
steps.helpDetail: Keep your feet up at the surface of the water, pointing in the direction of travel to bounce off rocks.

steps.instruction: Steer using your arms.
steps.helpDetail: Backpedal with your arms to slow your descent and angle your body 45-degrees toward the nearest safe eddy/shoreline.

steps.instruction: Flip and swim only at the end.
steps.helpDetail: Once you are out of the main current and near an eddy, flip onto your stomach and aggressively swim to shore.

techniques.name: River Strainer Survival
techniques.subtitle: Avoiding lethal downed trees

steps.instruction: Identify the threat.
steps.helpDetail: A strainer is a downed tree with branches under the water. Water flows through it, but solid objects (like you) are pinned against it and dragged under.

steps.instruction: Swim aggressively forward.
steps.helpDetail: If you cannot avoid a strainer, flip from your back onto your stomach immediately.

steps.instruction: Do NOT go under.
steps.helpDetail: Never try to dive under a strainer. You will be pinned by the branches.

steps.instruction: Launch yourself up.
steps.helpDetail: Swim as hard as possible toward the log. Just before impact, lunge up and out of the water, throwing your chest and arms over the top of the log.

steps.instruction: Crawl over the obstacle.
steps.helpDetail: Pull your legs up and scramble over the top of the tree frame to the safe downstream side.

techniques.name: Bear Attack Defense (Advanced)
techniques.subtitle: Defensive vs. Predatory behavior analysis

steps.instruction: Identify bear species.
steps.helpDetail: Brown/Grizzly bears usually attack defensively (to protect cubs/food). Black bears are more likely to attack predatorily, though attacks are rare.

steps.instruction: Identify attack intent.
steps.helpDetail: Defensive: Bear is huffing, popping jaw, ears back, charging but stopping. Predatory: Bear is silent, stalking you, head low, ears forward.

steps.instruction: Respond to DEFENSIVE.
steps.helpDetail: Play dead. Lie flat on your stomach, hands clasped behind your neck, legs spread wide to prevent being flipped over.

steps.instruction: Respond to PREDATORY.
steps.helpDetail: FIGHT BACK. Never play dead. Target the bear's eyes and nose with rocks, sticks, or bear spray. Do not climb a tree (black bears are excellent climbers).

techniques.name: Mountain Lion Encounters
techniques.subtitle: Intimidation and dominance assertion

steps.instruction: DO NOT RUN.
steps.helpDetail: Running triggers the predator chase instinct. A mountain lion will outrun you effortlessly.

steps.instruction: Maintain unbroken eye contact.
steps.helpDetail: Cougars are ambush predators. They rely on the element of surprise. If they know you see them, they often abort the attack.

steps.instruction: Make yourself massive.
steps.helpDetail: Open your jacket, raise your arms, and stand on higher ground if available. Do not crouch or bend over.

steps.instruction: Throw projectiles.
steps.helpDetail: Throw rocks or branches at the cat, aiming for the head. Yell aggressively and deeply. Do not use high-pitched shrieks.

steps.instruction: Fight back viciously if attacked.
steps.helpDetail: If it pounces, protect your neck and fight back. Use a knife, rock, or thumb gouge its eyes.

techniques.name: Vehicle Rollover Bracing
techniques.subtitle: Minimizing trauma during kinetic rotation

steps.instruction: Cross your arms.
steps.helpDetail: Hold your shoulders with the opposite hands, crossing them over your chest in an 'X'. This prevents arm flailing outside the window.

steps.instruction: Tuck your chin.
steps.helpDetail: Bury your chin tight into your chest. This prevents severe whiplash and cervical spine snapping.

steps.instruction: Press deeply into the seat.
steps.helpDetail: Use your legs to push your back deeply into the seat core. Do not lean forward.

steps.instruction: Exhale before impact.
steps.helpDetail: Lungs full of air can rupture under the massive blunt force compression of a crash. Let out a sharp breath immediately prior.

techniques.name: Submerged Vehicle Extrication
techniques.subtitle: Overcoming hydrostatic pressure

steps.instruction: Do NOT wait for pressure to slowly equalize.
steps.helpDetail: Waiting for the car to fill completely with water takes too long and induces panic. Act immediately.

steps.instruction: Unbuckle seatbelts.
steps.helpDetail: Unbuckle yourself first, then children. Leave the children strapped into their car seats initially—just unbuckle the belt holding the seat itself.

steps.instruction: Roll down windows fast.
steps.helpDetail: Power windows will work for 30-60 seconds underwater. Do this the exact second you hit the water.

steps.instruction: Break the glass if jammed.
steps.helpDetail: If windows fail, break a side window (not the windshield). Hit the bottom corner of the glass with a center punch, metal headrest peg, or spark plug debris.

steps.instruction: Swim out head first.
steps.helpDetail: Push children out ahead of you, then pull yourself out the window.

techniques.name: Avalanche Burial Survival
techniques.subtitle: Actions during and after avalanche burial

steps.instruction: Swim aggressively during the slide
steps.helpDetail: Use swimming motions to stay near the surface.

steps.instruction: Create an air pocket before debris settles
steps.helpDetail: Cup hands over mouth and nose as the snow stops moving.

steps.instruction: Determine which way is up
steps.helpDetail: Spit or let saliva drip. Gravity tells you which direction to dig.

steps.instruction: Conserve air
steps.helpDetail: Don't scream (wastes air). Dig toward the surface methodically. Your time is 15-30 minutes.

techniques.name: Tsunami Survival Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Recognizing and surviving tidal waves

steps.instruction: Recognize natural warnings
steps.helpDetail: Ocean pulls back dramatically, exposing seafloor. Loud roaring sound from ocean. Earthquake.

steps.instruction: Move to high ground IMMEDIATELY
steps.helpDetail: Go UP, not inland. A building's 3rd floor or a hill is safer than running horizontally.

steps.instruction: If caught in water, grab floating debris
steps.helpDetail: Climb onto anything buoyant. A door, table, or tree trunk.

steps.instruction: Expect multiple waves
steps.helpDetail: The first wave is often NOT the largest. Stay at altitude for hours.

techniques.name: Volcanic Ash Fall Survival
techniques.subtitle: Protecting yourself from volcanic ashfall

steps.instruction: Seal your shelter
steps.helpDetail: Close all windows and doors. Cover vents with wet cloth.

steps.instruction: Protect your lungs
steps.helpDetail: Breathe through a wet T-shirt or N95 mask. Volcanic ash is glass particles.

steps.instruction: Protect your eyes
steps.helpDetail: Wear sealed goggles. Ash scratches corneas instantly.

steps.instruction: Do not drive
steps.helpDetail: Ash clogs air filters and engine intakes. Visibility drops to zero.

techniques.name: Hurricane Shelter Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Surviving a direct hurricane hit

steps.instruction: Shelter in an interior room on lowest floor
steps.helpDetail: Away from windows. Bathrooms and closets provide extra structural support.

steps.instruction: Do NOT go outside during the eye
steps.helpDetail: The eye wall on the backside arrives suddenly with winds from the opposite direction.

steps.instruction: Store emergency water in bathtubs
steps.helpDetail: Fill all bathtubs and containers before the storm hits. Water service will fail.

steps.instruction: After the storm, avoid flood water
steps.helpDetail: Downed power lines, sewage, and chemical contamination make floodwater lethal.

techniques.name: Sandstorm Survival
techniques.subtitle: Protecting yourself in a desert sandstorm

steps.instruction: Shelter behind any windbreak
steps.helpDetail: Rock, vehicle, hill. Get on the leeward side.

steps.instruction: Cover nose, mouth, and eyes completely
steps.helpDetail: Sand damages lungs and eyes rapidly. Use a wet cloth for breathing.

steps.instruction: Turn your back to the wind
steps.helpDetail: Crouch low. Sand blasts at lower levels are more intense.

steps.instruction: Wait it out
steps.helpDetail: Most sandstorms last 1-3 hours. Stay put until visibility returns.

techniques.name: Flash Flood Vehicle Escape
techniques.subtitle: Surviving a vehicle caught in flash flooding

steps.instruction: Never drive through flowing water
steps.helpDetail: 6 inches can knock you down. 2 feet sweeps away vehicles.

steps.instruction: If water rises around your car, ESCAPE IMMEDIATELY
steps.helpDetail: Open the door or window before water reaches the bottom of the door.

steps.instruction: If water is too deep to open doors
steps.helpDetail: Break a side window. Use a spring-loaded center punch or your car headrest metal pegs.

steps.instruction: Move to the roof of the car
steps.helpDetail: If current prevents swimming, climb to the roof and signal for rescue.

techniques.name: Whiteout Navigation
techniques.subtitle: Moving safely in zero-visibility snow conditions

steps.instruction: STOP if possible
steps.helpDetail: Whiteout navigation is extremely dangerous. Shelter in place if you can.

steps.instruction: If you must move, use a rope team
steps.helpDetail: Spread 15 feet apart. The leader probes ahead with a pole.

steps.instruction: Use a compass bearing and pace count
steps.helpDetail: You cannot navigate by visual references. Dead reckoning is your only option.

steps.instruction: Beware of cliffs and crevasses
steps.helpDetail: In whiteout, you can walk off a cliff without seeing it. Probe EVERY step.

techniques.sourceName: FM 3-25.26 Map Reading and Land Navigation

techniques.name: Cold Water Swimming Survival
techniques.subtitle: Extending survival time in cold water

steps.instruction: Control breathing
steps.helpDetail: Cold water triggers gasp reflex. Focus on slow, deliberate breaths for the first 60 seconds.

steps.instruction: HELP position if alone
steps.helpDetail: Heat Escape Lessening Posture: knees to chest, arms folded across chest.

steps.instruction: Huddle if with others
steps.helpDetail: Face inward, arms around each other, children in the center.

steps.instruction: Do NOT swim unless shore is within 100m
steps.helpDetail: Swimming accelerates heat loss 30-50%. Float if rescue is expected.

techniques.name: Urban Survival After Disaster
techniques.subtitle: Navigate collapsed urban environment safely

steps.instruction: Assess structural stability before entering ANY building.
steps.helpDetail: Cracks in load-bearing walls, leaning structure, broken foundation = DO NOT ENTER.

steps.instruction: Locate water: hot water heaters (40-80 gallons), toilet tanks (not bowls), pipes.
steps.helpDetail: Hot water heater is the single biggest water reserve in any building. Open drain valve at bottom.

steps.instruction: Scavenge systematically: food, water, medicine, tools, clothing, communication devices.
steps.helpDetail: Pharmacies, grocery backrooms, and fire stations are high-value targets.

steps.instruction: Move at dawn. Stay off main roads — they become choke points. Use alleys and rooftops.
steps.helpDetail: In lawless post-disaster: travel in groups, avoid confrontation, and keep supplies hidden.

techniques.name: Vehicle Breakdown in Desert
techniques.subtitle: Stay with the vehicle — it's your biggest survival asset

steps.instruction: STAY WITH THE VEHICLE. It's visible to searchers and provides shade.
steps.helpDetail: Walking in desert is almost always fatal. Vehicles are found; people on foot are not.

steps.instruction: Create shade: open all doors, drape blankets/space blankets over the roof.
steps.helpDetail: Reduce solar heating. The vehicle interior can exceed 150°F.

steps.instruction: Ration water: drink what you need, don't sip. Your body can't store small sips.
steps.helpDetail: Signal with mirrors, horn, headlights, and tires (burn one for black smoke column).

steps.instruction: Energy budget: ALL activity in pre-dawn and post-dusk. No movement midday.
steps.helpDetail: Conserve water by minimizing sweating. A person at rest in shade uses 1/3 the water of someone walking.

techniques.name: Vehicle Breakdown in Winter
techniques.subtitle: Your car is a survival shelter — don't leave it

steps.instruction: STAY IN THE VEHICLE unless shelter is visible within 100 yards.
steps.helpDetail: Cars protect from wind, are visible to rescuers, and insulate better than you'd think.

steps.instruction: Run engine 10-15 minutes per hour for heat.
steps.helpDetail: Clear snow from exhaust pipe FIRST — CO poisoning kills silently. Crack a downwind window.

steps.instruction: Insulate: stuff seats with newspaper, floor mats on windows, huddle for warmth.
steps.helpDetail: Your body heat trapped in the car is significant. Exercise in place periodically to generate warmth.

steps.instruction: Signal: tie bright cloth to antenna, turn on hazard lights periodically, stomp SOS in snow.
steps.helpDetail: At night: dome light on intermittently. Conserve battery for signaling.

techniques.name: Nuclear Fallout Shelter-in-Place
techniques.subtitle: Maximize shielding from radioactive fallout

steps.instruction: Get inside the most massive structure available — concrete, brick, underground.
steps.helpDetail: Every inch of dense material between you and fallout reduces radiation. Basements are ideal.

steps.instruction: Seal all openings: tape windows, block vents, close all doors.
steps.helpDetail: Fallout is physical particles — prevent them from entering your air space.

steps.instruction: Stay sheltered for minimum 48 hours, ideally 2 weeks.
steps.helpDetail: Radiation decreases by 90% after 7 hours, 99% after 48 hours. Two weeks makes most areas safe to traverse briefly.

steps.instruction: If you were outside during the blast: remove ALL clothing, shower or wipe down thoroughly.
steps.helpDetail: Contaminated clothing accounts for 90% of external radiation exposure. Bag it and get it away from living space.

techniques.name: Earthquake Response (During)
techniques.subtitle: Drop, Cover, Hold On — do not run outside

steps.instruction: DROP to your hands and knees immediately.
steps.helpDetail: This prevents falling. Most earthquake injuries come from falling or being hit by objects, not building collapse.

steps.instruction: COVER your head and neck under a sturdy desk or table.
steps.helpDetail: If no table: against an interior wall, cover head with arms. Stay away from windows and exterior walls.

steps.instruction: HOLD ON to your shelter until shaking stops.
steps.helpDetail: Earthquakes can last 10-60 seconds. Furniture will slide — hold on and move with it.

steps.instruction: After shaking stops: expect aftershocks. Check for gas leaks (smell), structural damage, and injuries.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT use elevators. Do NOT light matches until gas leaks are ruled out. Exit only when safe.

techniques.name: Tsunami Warning Signs & Response
techniques.subtitle: Natural warnings that give you minutes to act

steps.instruction: Warning sign 1: earthquake you can feel while near the coast.
steps.helpDetail: If the ground shakes for more than 20 seconds near the ocean, move to high ground IMMEDIATELY without waiting for official warning.

steps.instruction: Warning sign 2: ocean recedes dramatically, exposing the seafloor.
steps.helpDetail: This is NOT a curiosity — the water is being pulled into the approaching wave. You have minutes.

steps.instruction: RUN TO HIGH GROUND. At least 100 feet elevation or 2 miles inland.
steps.helpDetail: Go UP, not just away from the coast. Drive if roads are clear. Run if they're not.

steps.instruction: Multiple waves will arrive over hours — the first is often NOT the largest.
steps.helpDetail: Do not return to the coast for at least 12 hours or until authorities give the all-clear.

techniques.name: Tornado Shelter in Open Field
techniques.subtitle: Survive a tornado with no building nearby

steps.instruction: If you can drive away at right angles to the tornado's path, DO IT.
steps.helpDetail: Tornadoes generally move SW to NE. Drive due south or southeast.

steps.instruction: If trapped: find a ditch, culvert, or low depression.
steps.helpDetail: Lie flat face-down. Get as low as possible. Tornado debris field is typically above ground level.

steps.instruction: Protect your head and neck with arms, bag, or any object.
steps.helpDetail: Flying debris is the primary killer. Head and spinal protection is crucial.

steps.instruction: DO NOT shelter under highway overpasses — wind accelerates through them.
steps.helpDetail: Overpasses create wind-tunnel effects. This is one of the most dangerous tornado myths.

techniques.name: Hurricane Preparation & Survival
techniques.subtitle: 72-hour advance preparation protocol

steps.instruction: Board windows, secure outdoor items, fill bathtubs with water.
steps.helpDetail: Plywood or storm shutters on all windows. Flying debris is the #1 danger.

steps.instruction: Stock 1 gallon of water per person per day for 7 days.
steps.helpDetail: Municipal water may be contaminated for days post-storm.

steps.instruction: Shelter in an interior room on the lowest floor during the storm.
steps.helpDetail: Bathroom or closet — small rooms with reinforcing walls. Stay away from windows.

steps.instruction: DO NOT go outside during the eye — the backside hits from the opposite direction with no warning.
steps.helpDetail: The calm of the eye is temporary. The wind returns suddenly from the opposite direction, pushing storm surge back.

techniques.name: Flash Flood Survival
techniques.subtitle: React in seconds — 6 inches of water knocks you down

steps.instruction: Recognize warning signs: distant rumbling, sudden rise in water level, debris in stream.
steps.helpDetail: Flash floods arrive in minutes. Don't wait to confirm — ACT.

steps.instruction: Move to high ground IMMEDIATELY. Do not try to cross flowing water.
steps.helpDetail: 6 inches of moving water knocks an adult down. 2 feet carries a car away.

steps.instruction: If trapped in a vehicle: abandon it if water is rising and you can reach high ground.
steps.helpDetail: If trapped: climb onto the roof. Do NOT stay inside a submerging vehicle.

steps.instruction: After flood: avoid walking in floodwater — contains sewage, chemicals, and hidden hazards.
steps.helpDetail: Downed power lines in floodwater are invisible killers. Assume all floodwater is contaminated.

techniques.name: Snake Encounter Prevention
techniques.subtitle: Avoid bites through behavior modification

steps.instruction: Watch where you step and put your hands. 80% of bites are on hands and feet.
steps.helpDetail: Step ON logs, not over them. Look before reaching into crevices or brush.

steps.instruction: Wear boots and long pants in snake country.
steps.helpDetail: Ankle-high boots prevent the majority of venomous bites. Thick fabric absorbs some venom.

steps.instruction: At night: use a light and clear your sleeping area before lying down.
steps.helpDetail: Snakes are attracted to warmth — they may seek body heat at night.

steps.instruction: If you see a snake: freeze, then slowly back away. Most snakes strike within 1/3 of their body length.
steps.helpDetail: Snakes don't chase humans. If you give them an escape route, they'll take it. Stand still, let it leave.

techniques.name: Heat Exhaustion Emergency Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Stop progression to heat stroke — which is fatal

steps.instruction: Recognize: heavy sweating, weakness, cold/clammy skin, nausea, fast weak pulse.
steps.helpDetail: Heat exhaustion = body's cooling system is overwhelmed but still functioning.

steps.instruction: Move to shade or coolest available area immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Any shade is dramatically cooler than direct sun.

steps.instruction: Remove excess clothing. Apply cool water to skin, fan the patient.
steps.helpDetail: Wet cloths on neck, armpits, and groin cool core temperature fastest.

steps.instruction: If patient stops sweating, becomes confused, or has hot dry skin: this is HEAT STROKE. Emergency.
steps.helpDetail: Heat stroke = cooling has FAILED. Core temp >104°F. Cool aggressively with ice if available. Evacuate immediately. Mortality is high without rapid cooling.

techniques.name: Landslide & Mudflow Warning Signs
techniques.subtitle: Seconds of warning can save your life

steps.instruction: Warning signs: cracks in ground, tilting trees/poles, bulging ground, new springs or seeps.
steps.helpDetail: These indicate soil is moving. Evacuate uphill or perpendicular to the flow path.

steps.instruction: During heavy rain on steep slopes: listen for rumbling like a freight train.
steps.helpDetail: Debris flows travel 20-40 mph. If you hear it from upstream, move to high ground NOW.

steps.instruction: Move perpendicular to the flow direction — do not run downhill ahead of it.
steps.helpDetail: You cannot outrun a mudflow. Move sideways out of the channel or valley.

steps.instruction: After: avoid the area — secondary slides are common.
steps.helpDetail: Do not cross fresh mudflows — they can reactivate. Report downed utilities from safe distance.

techniques.name: Wildfire Approach Assessment
techniques.subtitle: Determine if a wildfire threatens your position

steps.instruction: Watch smoke column: leaning toward you = fire approaching. Vertical column = low wind.
steps.helpDetail: Dark smoke = active burning. White smoke = steam from wet fuel or fire dying down.

steps.instruction: Wind direction determines fire spread — fire moves with the wind.
steps.helpDetail: Uphill spread is fastest. Fire doubles speed for every 20° of slope.

steps.instruction: Identify escape routes NOW before you need them — two routes minimum.
steps.helpDetail: Roads, rivers, rocky areas, previously burned zones. PLAN before panic.

steps.instruction: If fire is within 1 mile and approaching: evacuate immediately. Don't wait to see if it changes direction.
steps.helpDetail: Fires can accelerate from walking speed to 70 mph in seconds during a blow-up. Leave early, leave often.

techniques.name: Crocodile/Alligator Territory Safety
techniques.subtitle: Survive in crocodilian habitat

steps.instruction: Assume any water body in crocodile territory contains crocodiles.
steps.helpDetail: Freshwater AND saltwater. Estuaries, rivers, lakes, even coastal ocean. They travel between water bodies.

steps.instruction: Never approach the water's edge closer than 5 meters.
steps.helpDetail: Crocodiles can lunge their entire body length from water in under 1 second.

steps.instruction: Camp 50+ meters from water. Store food away from camp AND away from water.
steps.helpDetail: Crocs investigate smells. Food scraps attract them to your camp.

steps.instruction: If attacked: target the eyes and nostrils — only vulnerable points.
steps.helpDetail: On land, run in a straight line — the 'zigzag' advice is a myth. In water, fight back ferociously. Playing dead doesn't work with crocodiles.

techniques.name: Navigating Jellyfish-Infested Water
techniques.subtitle: Cross water safely when jellyfish are present

steps.instruction: Cover all exposed skin: clothing, wetsuit, even plastic bags over limbs.
steps.helpDetail: Any fabric barrier blocks nematocyst penetration.

steps.instruction: Swim slowly and deliberately — don't splash.
steps.helpDetail: Thrashing movement pushes jellyfish into you and breaks tentacle fragments.

steps.instruction: If stung while swimming: don't panic. Exit water calmly.
steps.helpDetail: Panicking leads to further stings and drowning risk.

steps.instruction: Treatment after exit: vinegar for most species, hot water for Portuguese man-o-war.
steps.helpDetail: Multiple stings from box jellyfish can be fatal — exit water immediately and treat aggressively.

techniques.sourceName: FM 3-25.26 Map Reading and Land Navigation

techniques.name: Volcanic Eruption Response
techniques.subtitle: Survive lava flows, pyroclastic flows, and lahars

steps.instruction: Pyroclastic flow (superheated gas cloud) moves 400+ mph — you CANNOT outrun it.
steps.helpDetail: If you see one: get behind the largest solid structure available. Mountains, hills, and valleys may divert flow.

steps.instruction: Lava flow: generally slow (walking pace). Move perpendicular to the flow direction.
steps.helpDetail: Lava follows topography downhill. Move uphill or laterally to escape.

steps.instruction: Lahar (volcanic mudflow): triggered by eruption melting glaciers. Moves 40+ mph down valleys.
steps.helpDetail: Stay out of valleys and channels downstream from the volcano. Move to high ground.

steps.instruction: Ash fall: shelter indoors. Wet ash on roofs is extremely heavy — clear it before collapse.
steps.helpDetail: Wet volcanic ash weighs ~100 lbs per cubic foot. 6 inches of wet ash can collapse a roof.

techniques.name: Spider Bite Prevention in Camp
techniques.subtitle: Reduce spider encounters in your living area

steps.instruction: Shake out ALL clothing and boots before putting them on.
steps.helpDetail: Spiders seek dark, tight spaces. Your shoes are perfect spider habitat.

steps.instruction: Keep sleeping area clear of ground debris. Sleep off the ground if possible.
steps.helpDetail: Elevated sleeping dramatically reduces spider and scorpion encounters.

steps.instruction: Seal tent/shelter openings. Tuck pants into socks.
steps.helpDetail: Block entry paths. Most bites happen when spiders are trapped against skin inside clothing.

steps.instruction: Check under logs, rocks, and debris before grabbing with bare hands.
steps.helpDetail: Brown recluse and black widow both hide in dark, undisturbed spaces. One glance before grabbing prevents 90% of bites.

techniques.name: Tundra Survival Essentials
techniques.subtitle: Above treeline — wind and exposure are the primary threats

steps.instruction: Wind is the primary killer — wind chill drops perceived temperature 40+ degrees.
steps.helpDetail: Wind is the primary killer — wind chill drops perceived temperature 40+ degrees.

steps.instruction: Build windbreaks from snow blocks or dig into snowdrifts.
steps.helpDetail: Build windbreaks from snow blocks or dig into snowdrifts.

steps.instruction: Water is everywhere but frozen — melt snow, don't eat it directly (lowers core temp).
steps.helpDetail: Water is everywhere but frozen — melt snow, don't eat it directly (lowers core temp).

steps.instruction: Navigation: no trees for landmarks, use snow drifts (wind-shaped), sun, and stars.
steps.helpDetail: Food: ground squirrels, ptarmigan, Arctic hare, lichen, berries under snow. Travel on frozen rivers — but test ice thickness: 4 inches minimum for walking.

techniques.name: Tropical Jungle Survival
techniques.subtitle: Everything is wet, everything bites, everything grows

steps.instruction: Water is abundant but contaminated — ALWAYS treat.
steps.helpDetail: Water is abundant but contaminated — ALWAYS treat.

steps.instruction: Movement is extremely slow: 1 mile per hour through thick jungle.
steps.helpDetail: Movement is extremely slow: 1 mile per hour through thick jungle.

steps.instruction: Follow streams downstream to settlements.
steps.helpDetail: Follow streams downstream to settlements.

steps.instruction: Insect protection is critical — malaria and dengue kill more than anything in the jungle.
steps.helpDetail: Keep feet dry: trench foot develops in 24 hours. Camp on ridgelines, not valleys (cold air, flooding, insects pool in valleys). Bamboo provides water (cut fresh green internodes), building material, and cooking vessels.

techniques.name: Mountain Survival & Alpine Zone
techniques.subtitle: High altitude, weather exposure, and difficult terrain

steps.instruction: Altitude sickness affects everyone above 8,000 feet — ascend slowly, 1,000 feet per day max.
steps.helpDetail: Altitude sickness affects everyone above 8,000 feet — ascend slowly, 1,000 feet per day max.

steps.instruction: Weather changes in minutes — carry layers and rain protection always.
steps.helpDetail: Weather changes in minutes — carry layers and rain protection always.

steps.instruction: Lightning risk above treeline — descend below ridgelines during storms.
steps.helpDetail: Lightning risk above treeline — descend below ridgelines during storms.

steps.instruction: Water from snowmelt is abundant and relatively clean.
steps.helpDetail: Movement: traverse slopes at angles, avoid cornices, test snow bridges before crossing. Rockfall risk increases with afternoon heat — travel early morning. Food is scarce above treeline — descend to forest for foraging.

techniques.name: Open Ocean Survival
techniques.subtitle: Adrift at sea — water, shade, and signaling are everything

steps.instruction: Do NOT drink seawater — it accelerates dehydration and death.
steps.helpDetail: Collect rain in any container

steps.instruction: Solar still from seawater if materials available.
steps.helpDetail: Shade is critical — sunburn and heat stroke kill faster than dehydration at sea

steps.instruction: Signal: mirror during day, light at night.
steps.helpDetail: Any visual anomaly attracts attention

steps.instruction: Fish: improvise hooks, trail bait behind raft.
steps.helpDetail: Sea turtles, seabirds are edible. Raw fish flesh contains fresh water. Do NOT swim unless shore is visible — sharks, hypothermia, and current separation from raft.

techniques.name: Urban Post-Collapse Survival
techniques.subtitle: Surviving in a city after infrastructure failure

steps.instruction: Water: hot water heaters (40-80 gal per building), toilet tanks (not bowls), swimming pools (treat first).
steps.helpDetail: Water: hot water heaters (40-80 gal per building), toilet tanks (not bowls), swimming pools (treat first).

steps.instruction: Food: grocery stores, restaurants, vending machines — systematically clear them.
steps.helpDetail: Food: grocery stores, restaurants, vending machines — systematically clear them.

steps.instruction: Security: travel in groups, designated watchperson, avoid main roads at night.
steps.helpDetail: Security: travel in groups, designated watchperson, avoid main roads at night.

steps.instruction: Shelter: choose upper floors (flooding), interior rooms (sniper protection), buildings with clear sight lines.
steps.helpDetail: Communication: battery-powered radios, notes on doors for other survivors, rendezvous points. Scavenge: hardware stores for tools, pharmacies for medicine, fire stations for gear.

techniques.name: Thunderstorm Safety Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Systematic threat assessment and protective actions

steps.instruction: 30/30 rule: if flash-to-bang is under 30 seconds, take shelter immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Each 5-second count = 1 mile distance. Under 30 seconds = within 6 miles.

steps.instruction: Best shelter: hard-topped vehicle or substantial building.
steps.helpDetail: NOT open shelters, tents, or under isolated trees.

steps.instruction: If caught in open: get to lowest point, crouch on balls of feet, minimize contact with ground.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT lie flat — ground current from nearby strikes travels through larger contact area.

steps.instruction: Stay sheltered 30 minutes after LAST thunder.
steps.helpDetail: Many lightning deaths occur as storms appear to be leaving. The trailing edge is as dangerous as the leading edge.

techniques.name: Ice Travel Safety Assessment
techniques.subtitle: Determine if ice is safe to cross — thickness and quality matter

steps.instruction: Minimum ice thickness: 4 inches clear ice for walking. 5 inches for snowmobile. 8-12 inches for vehicles.
steps.helpDetail: WHITE ice or snow ice has half the strength of clear blue/black ice.

steps.instruction: Test with an ice chisel or axe every 50 feet as you cross.
steps.helpDetail: Never assume ice is uniformly thick. Current, springs, and inflow areas weaken ice.

steps.instruction: Carry ice picks around your neck and a throw rope.
steps.helpDetail: If you break through: kick feet behind you and pull onto ice with picks. Roll away from the hole.

steps.instruction: Avoid: river bends, bridges, areas near docks, and late-season ice.
steps.helpDetail: Moving water under ice prevents thickening. Late-season 'candle ice' can be 12 inches thick but has zero strength.

techniques.name: Sinkhole Recognition & Avoidance
techniques.subtitle: Identify at-risk terrain before the ground collapses

steps.instruction: Warning signs: circular depressions, cracks in ground or pavement, leaning trees/fences.
steps.helpDetail: Sinkholes form in limestone and karite terrain where underground water dissolves rock.

steps.instruction: Fresh sinkholes: bare earth in a circular pattern, slumping edges, water draining into the ground.
steps.helpDetail: If water suddenly drains from a pond or stream, a sinkhole may be forming.

steps.instruction: Camp on solid bedrock when possible — avoid karst terrain (limestone with caves).
steps.helpDetail: If you can see cave openings or springs, the area is sinkhole-prone.

steps.instruction: If the ground begins to subside: move away from the center IMMEDIATELY.
steps.helpDetail: Sinkholes can open in seconds. The center drops first, then the edges follow.

techniques.name: Dust Storm Health Hazards
techniques.subtitle: Manage respiratory and eye protection during extended dust events

steps.instruction: Cover mouth and nose with a damp cloth — dust particles under 10 microns reach deep lung tissue.
steps.helpDetail: Fine dust causes silicosis with chronic exposure. Acute exposure causes asthma-like symptoms.

steps.instruction: Goggles or any eye protection: dust contains silica and fungi (Valley Fever in US Southwest).
steps.helpDetail: Coccidioidomycosis (Valley Fever) is inhaled from desert dust. Can cause severe pneumonia.

steps.instruction: Seal shelter openings: hang wet blankets over doors and windows.
steps.helpDetail: Dust infiltrates through smallest gaps.

steps.instruction: After dust storm: clean all water containers, food surfaces, and equipment thoroughly.
steps.helpDetail: Dust contaminates water supplies and can coat food with harmful minerals.

techniques.name: Fire Ant Attack Response
techniques.subtitle: Manage mass fire ant envenomation — thousands of simultaneous stings

steps.instruction: Brush ants off QUICKLY — they coordinate stinging in response to a chemical alarm signal.
steps.helpDetail: Fire ants bite to anchor, then sting repeatedly with their abdomen. Each ant stings multiple times.

steps.instruction: Move away from the mound. Remove all clothing and check skin for remaining ants.
steps.helpDetail: Ants can be inside clothing and continue stinging for hours.

steps.instruction: Cold compress on sting areas. Antihistamine for itching.
steps.helpDetail: Each sting becomes a white pustule within 24 hours. Do NOT pop them — infection risk.

steps.instruction: Watch for anaphylaxis if hundreds of stings: difficulty breathing, swelling, dizziness.
steps.helpDetail: Mass envenomation from fire ants can trigger anaphylaxis in sensitized individuals. Epinephrine if available.

techniques.name: Moose Encounter Safety
techniques.subtitle: Most dangerous large animal in North America by injury count

steps.instruction: Moose are NOT docile — they attack more people annually than bears and wolves combined.
steps.helpDetail: During rutting season (fall) and when cows have calves (spring), they are extremely aggressive.

steps.instruction: Warning signs: ears pinned back, hair standing up on neck, licking lips, stomping feet.
steps.helpDetail: These are imminent-charge signals. BACK AWAY.

steps.instruction: If a moose charges: run. Use a tree or vehicle as a barrier.
steps.helpDetail: Unlike bears, moose will NOT chase you far. Their charge is a bluff or short rush.

steps.instruction: If knocked down: curl into a ball, protect your head.
steps.helpDetail: Moose stomp with front hooves. Once you stop moving and don't appear threatening, they usually leave.

techniques.name: Wasp & Hornet Nest Avoidance
techniques.subtitle: Prevent mass stinging events in camp and on trail

steps.instruction: Scan ahead on trail: look for paper nests in trees, ground holes with wasp traffic, and hollow logs.
steps.helpDetail: Ground-nesting yellowjackets are the most common source of mass stinging: disturbed by footsteps.

steps.instruction: If wasps begin investigating you: remain still and move away slowly.
steps.helpDetail: Rapid movement triggers attack response. Wasps are attracted to dark colors and erratic movement.

steps.instruction: If stung once, leave the area quickly — the sting releases alarm pheromones attracting more wasps.
steps.helpDetail: The alarm pheromone is on the sting site. Moving away from the nest reduces additional stings.

steps.instruction: If swarmed: RUN. Cover your face and run in a straight line for 200+ yards.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT jump in water — they wait for you to surface. Run to an enclosed shelter if possible.

techniques.name: Altitude Acclimatization Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Prevent altitude sickness through proper ascent scheduling

steps.instruction: Above 8,000 feet: sleep no more than 1,000 feet higher than the previous night.
