steps.helpDetail: 'Climb high, sleep low.' You can climb to higher elevation during the day but return to lower camp to sleep.

steps.instruction: Stay hydrated: 3-4 liters per day. Dehydration mimics and worsens altitude sickness.
steps.helpDetail: High altitude is extremely dehydrating — dry air and increased respiration rate pull water from your body.

steps.instruction: Eat a high-carbohydrate diet at altitude.
steps.helpDetail: Carbs require less oxygen to metabolize than fat or protein. Your body's preferred altitude fuel.

steps.instruction: Rest day every 3,000 feet of elevation gain.
steps.helpDetail: Your body needs 24-48 hours to produce additional red blood cells for each altitude step. Rushing causes AMS, HACE, or HAPE.

techniques.name: Desert Water Rationing
techniques.subtitle: The physiological truth about rationing water in extreme heat.

steps.instruction: Do not ration your water.
steps.helpDetail: The military axiom is 'Ration sweat, not water.' Drink when you are thirsty. Water sitting in a canteen does not prevent heat stroke; only water in your body sweating out can cool you.

steps.instruction: Rest strictly during the day.
steps.helpDetail: Do absolutely nothing between 10:00 AM and 4:00 PM. Find deep shade, sit quietly, and do not digest heavy protein foods (digestion requires massive amounts of water).

steps.instruction: Travel exclusively at night.
steps.helpDetail: Temperatures plummet at night. You can cover 3-4 times the distance on the same amount of water by moving from dusk till dawn.

steps.instruction: Keep your mouth shut.
steps.helpDetail: Breathe strictly through your nose. Keep your clothing on (it traps a micro-layer of humid sweat against your skin, slowing the evaporation rate).

techniques.name: Snow Blindness Prevention
techniques.subtitle: Protecting the retinas from severe UV burns.

steps.instruction: Recognize the massive UV threat.
steps.helpDetail: Snow reflects up to 80% of UV radiation. Even on heavily overcast, 'whiteout' days, you are receiving a double-dose of UV radiation into your eyes.

steps.instruction: Do not squint; it is not enough.
steps.helpDetail: Squinting does not block the ambient UV bouncing upward from the snow beneath your chin.

steps.instruction: Carve Inuit-style slit goggles.
steps.helpDetail: Find a piece of wood, bark, or dark plastic. Cut a notch for your nose, and carve two horizontal slits NO WIDER THAN a knife blade. Tie them tightly against your face with cordage.

steps.instruction: Charcoal the ocular cavities.
steps.helpDetail: Take crushed, blackened charcoal from the fire and rub it heavily under your eyes and on your nose. This absorbs the blinding glare bouncing up into the goggles.

techniques.name: Jungle River Crossing
techniques.subtitle: Navigating slow-moving, biologically hazardous waterways.

steps.instruction: Assess the crossing.
steps.helpDetail: Do not swim unless absolutely forced. Jungle rivers hide submerged logs (strainers), caimans/crocs, piranhas, and severe parasitic loads (e.g., Schistosomiasis).

steps.instruction: Check for the deepest, narrowest point vs shallow, wide.
steps.helpDetail: Wide, shallow crossings are generally safer but expose you to stingrays or submerged snakes. Probe the bottom with a long staff before every step.

steps.instruction: Construct a flotation aid.
steps.helpDetail: Bundle dried bamboo, balsa wood, or an inflated waterproof bag inside your pants (tied off at the ankles). Never swim with a backpack strapped to both shoulders; release the sternum and waist straps first.

steps.instruction: Dry off immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Shed all wet clothing immediately upon crossing. Leech removal (using salt, fire, or fingernails, never pulling forcibly) must happen at this moment to prevent severe bleeding/infection.

articles.title: Desert Survival Principles
articles.body:
The desert kills through dehydration and heat. Everything else is secondary.

The Big Three rules:
1. Shade is your primary need (shelter from sun)
2. Water is your second need
3. Everything else can wait

Body mechanics in heat:
- Resting in shade: 1 quart water per day to survive
- Walking at night: 1 gallon per night
- Walking in daytime: 1 quart per HOUR (unsustainable)

Common mistakes:
- Walking during the day (dehydrates 3× faster)
- Rationing water (drink when thirsty — always)
- Removing clothing (exposed skin dehydrates faster)
- Following mirages (heat shimmer, not water)

Desert navigation:
- Sand dunes trend perpendicular to prevailing wind
- Vegetation increases toward water
- Roads and power lines lead to civilization
- Aircraft contrails indicate flight paths between cities

articles.title: Arctic & Cold Weather Survival
articles.body:
Cold kills through hypothermia, which is insidious. You don't realize it's happening until you can't think clearly enough to act.

The insulation hierarchy:
- Still air is the best insulator
- Layering creates pockets of still air
- Cotton kills (absorbs water, loses insulation)
- Wool retains warmth when wet
- Synthetic insulation dries fastest

Caloric demands in extreme cold:
- Shivering burns 400-600 calories per hour
- Cold weather activity: 4,000-6,000 calories per day needed
- Without adequate calories, hypothermia accelerates

Key principles:
- Stay DRY at all costs — wetness = death
- Ventilate to prevent sweating inside layers
- Keep extremities protected (fingers, toes, ears, nose)
- Never sit directly on snow — insulate from ground
- Eat before sleeping — digestion generates heat

Wind chill: 10°F with 30mph wind has the same cooling effect as -33°F in calm air. Wind protection is as important as insulation.

articles.title: Tropical & Jungle Survival
articles.body:
The jungle provides everything for survival — and everything that can kill you.

Advantages:
- Abundant water (rainfall, streams, plants)
- Abundant food (plants, insects, fish)
- Unlimited building materials
- Shelter from wind

Dangers:
- Infections: every cut becomes infected within 24 hours in tropical humidity
- Parasites: waterborne and insect-borne diseases are the primary threat
- Dehydration: despite humidity, you sweat heavily and lose electrolytes
- Immersion foot: constant wet feet leads to tissue breakdown
- Predators: minimal risk compared to disease

Medical priorities:
1. Keep wounds clean and dry (impossible but try)
2. Use mosquito netting religiously (malaria kills)
3. Dry your feet completely once per day
4. Boil ALL water (jungle water has the highest parasite load)
5. Check body for leeches and ticks daily

Navigation: Following water downstream is the most reliable method to reach civilization in tropical environments.

articles.title: Mountain Hazard Awareness
articles.body:
Mountains compress multiple environments into short vertical distances. You can go from summer to winter in 3,000 feet of elevation.

Main hazards by altitude:
- Below treeline: exposure, getting lost, streams/rivers
- At treeline: lightning, wind, sudden weather
- Above treeline: altitude sickness, falls, avalanche, hypothermia

Weather changes:
- Mountains create their own weather. Clear mornings can become violent storms by 2 PM.
- Temperature drops approximately 3.5°F per 1,000 feet of elevation gain
- Start early, summit by noon, descend before afternoon storms

Avalanche awareness:
- Slopes 30-45° are highest risk
- New snow on old snow (weak layer) is the primary trigger
- Convex slopes are more dangerous than concave
- If buried: create air pocket in front of face, spit to determine which way is up (gravity), dig in the opposite direction

Falls:
- The #1 killer in mountains is falling
- Self-arrest with ice axe: roll onto stomach, dig pick into slope
- Three points of contact on exposed terrain at all times

articles.title: Ocean & Coastal Survival
articles.body:
Survival at sea is one of the most challenging environments. Your three enemies: dehydration, hypothermia, and sun exposure.

Survival priorities at sea:
1. Stay afloat (PFD, debris, HELP position)
2. Protection from elements (shade from sun, insulate from cold water)
3. Fresh water (rain collection is your only reliable source)
4. Signaling (mirror, dye, flares, electronics)
5. Food (last on the list — you can survive weeks without food)

Cold water immersion survival:
- 50°F water: 1-3 hours
- 60°F water: 3-12 hours
- 70°F water: 12-40 hours
- 80°F water: indefinite (but sunburn and dehydration kill)

If in a life raft:
- Stay dry if possible (wet clothing in the open = hypothermia)
- Collect rainwater in every possible surface
- Fish for food: trailing a line behind the raft works
- Signal EVERY aircraft and vessel
- Maintain morale: routine, tasks, hope

Shark awareness: sharks rarely attack humans. Avoid splashing, bleeding in water, and dawn/dusk swimming. If approached, maintain eye contact and back away slowly.

articles.title: Flood & Water Emergency Preparedness
articles.body:
Floods are the #1 natural disaster killer worldwide. Most flood deaths are from vehicles driving into floodwater.

Critical rules:
- Turn around, don't drown: NEVER drive through flooded roads
- 6 inches of water knocks you down
- 12 inches of water carries away a car
- 2 feet of water carries away an SUV/truck

Flash flood indicators:
- Heavy rain upstream (you may not see it locally)
- Rising water in streams/creeks
- Roaring sound from upstream
- Muddy water where it was clear
- Debris floating downstream

If trapped by rising water:
- Go to the highest accessible point
- Signal with lights, bright clothing, mirrors
- Do NOT enter floodwater to swim
- If swept away: float on back, feet downstream
- Grab any stable object and climb above water

After flood:
- Never drink floodwater (sewage, chemicals, debris)
- Avoid walking through standing water (contamination, hidden holes, downed power lines)
- Check structural integrity before entering buildings

articles.title: Multi-Hazard Emergency Planning
articles.body:
Most people prepare for one type of disaster and ignore everything else. True preparedness is hazard-agnostic.

Universal survival priorities (regardless of disaster type):
1. Immediate safety (get out of danger)
2. Medical care (treat injuries)
3. Shelter (protection from elements)
4. Water (within 3 days)
5. Communication (contact help)
6. Food (within 3 weeks)

Common across ALL disasters:
- Communication systems fail first (cell towers)
- Supply chains break within 72 hours
- Emergency services are overwhelmed
- Panic causes more casualties than the event itself
- Those who plan survive; those who don't become victims

The 72-hour rule:
- After ANY disaster, assume no help for 72 hours minimum
- Self-sufficiency for 3 days is the baseline
- Urban areas may take longer due to population density
- Rural areas may take longer due to access difficulty

Every household should have: water (1 gallon per person per day), food (non-perishable), prescriptions (30-day supply), cash, documents (in waterproof bag), first aid, flashlight, and radio.

articles.title: Climate Adaptation in Survival
articles.body:
Your body adapts to climate, but it takes time. Understanding acclimatization saves lives.

Heat acclimatization:
- Takes 7-14 days of gradual exposure
- Sweat production increases and becomes more dilute
- Heart rate decreases for same work level
- Core temperature rise is smaller
- Risk: pushing hard before acclimatization causes heat stroke

Cold acclimatization:
- Takes 2-4 weeks
- Blood flow to extremities improves (reduced vasoconstriction)
- Shivering threshold decreases
- Non-shivering thermogenesis (brown fat activation) increases
- Caloric needs increase 20-50%

Altitude acclimatization:
- Takes 3-5 days per 3,000 feet above 8,000 feet
- Red blood cell production increases
- Ventilation rate increases
- Risk: ascending too fast causes AMS, HACE, or HAPE
- The climb high, sleep low rule: ascend during the day, descend to sleep

Critical principle: you cannot rush acclimatization. It is a physiological process, not a mental one. Willpower does not accelerate it.

articles.title: Urban Disaster Navigation
articles.body:
Cities present unique challenges in disasters: collapsed structures, broken utilities, panicked crowds, and limited escape routes.

Urban-specific hazards:
- Falling glass from high-rises (up to 2 blocks away)
- Gas leaks from broken mains (explosion risk)
- Flooding from broken water mains
- Electrical hazards from downed wires
- Structural collapse (aftershocks, fire, water damage)
- Civil unrest (desperation sets in after 72 hours)

Navigation in damaged cities:
- Know your neighborhood's street grid before disaster
- Identify 3 evacuation routes from your home
- Avoid highway overpasses (collapse risk)
- Move perpendicular to windward side of fires
- Follow rivers downstream toward larger settlements

Urban resources often overlooked:
- Fire stations have tools, water, and communication
- Schools often serve as shelters
- Hospitals have backup power and supplies
- Hardware stores have survival-critical materials
- Parking garages provide structural shelter

The golden rule of urban disaster: get out early or shelter in place. The worst position is trying to evacuate when everyone else is.

articles.title: Altitude Sickness: From Mild to Fatal
articles.body:
Altitude sickness is progressive and predictable. Understanding the stages can save your life.

Stage 1 — Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS): 8,000-12,000 feet
- Headache (primary symptom)
- Nausea, loss of appetite
- Fatigue, weakness
- Dizziness
- Difficulty sleeping
Treatment: rest at same altitude, hydrate, ibuprofen. Resolves in 24-48 hours if you don't ascend.

Stage 2 — HACE (High Altitude Cerebral Edema): 12,000+ feet
- Severe headache unresponsive to medication
- Confusion, disorientation
- Loss of coordination (ataxia)
- Irrational behavior
- Hallucinations
Treatment: IMMEDIATE descent. This is a medical emergency. Fatal within 24 hours without descent.

Stage 3 — HAPE (High Altitude Pulmonary Edema): 8,000+ feet
- Persistent dry cough, then wet cough
- Breathlessness at rest
- Gurgling sounds when breathing
- Blue/gray lips and fingernails
- Pink, frothy sputum
Treatment: IMMEDIATE descent + supplemental oxygen. Fatal within hours if untreated.

Prevention: ascend slowly, hydrate, descend at first sign of worsening symptoms.

articles.title: Jungle Survival Medicine
articles.body:
The tropical jungle is the most medically hostile environment for survival.

Top jungle medical threats:
1. Dehydration/heat stroke (primary killer)
2. Infected wounds (tropical ulcers form in 24 hours)
3. Mosquito-borne disease (malaria, dengue)
4. Waterborne parasites (giardia, amoeba)
5. Venomous bites (snakes, spiders, scorpions)

Wound care in the jungle:
- ANY wound, no matter how small, must be cleaned immediately
- Wash with boiled water and natural antiseptics (tree tea oil plants, honey)
- Keep wounds open and draining — closing wounds in tropical conditions traps bacteria
- Change dressings twice daily, inspect for redness spreading, pus, or odor
- Tropical ulcers can become systemic infections within 48 hours

Natural jungle remedies:
- Aloe-type plants: apply gel to burns and wounds
- Honey: natural antibacterial (if available from wild bees)
- Charcoal: crushed and mixed with water for poison ingestion
- Citrus: natural vitamin C prevents scurvy on extended survival
- Turmeric root: anti-inflammatory, apply paste to wounds

Water discipline: in jungle, you need 1-2 liters per HOUR of movement. Plan routes around water sources.

articles.title: Flash Flood: Understanding the Deadliest Weather Killer
articles.body:
Flash floods kill more people annually than any other weather event. Understanding them is critical.

What makes flash floods deadly:
- Speed: water can rise 10+ feet in minutes
- Force: 6 inches of moving water knocks down an adult
- Invisibility: caused by rain miles away that you can't see
- Terrain multiplier: narrow canyons funnel and amplify water

Where flash floods occur:
- Slot canyons (most dangerous — vertical walls, no escape)
- Dry riverbeds (arroyos, wadis) in desert and arid regions
- Mountain stream valleys after heavy upstream rain
- Urban areas with poor drainage
- Below dam spillways

Warning signs:
1. Rumbling sound upstream (like a freight train)
2. Sudden increase in water turbidity (muddy water)
3. Floating debris in rising water
4. Dark clouds or heavy rain visible upstream
5. Rapid water level change in streams

Survival rules:
- Never camp below the high-water mark (look for debris lines on canyon walls)
- Never cross flooded roadways (2 feet of water floats most vehicles)
- Always have an uphill escape route planned
- In slot canyons, check weather forecasts for the entire watershed, not just your location
- If caught: climb, don't run. Vertical is safer than horizontal.

articles.title: Cave Use in Survival: Complete Guide
articles.body:
Caves provide excellent shelter but come with unique hazards.

Cave advantages:
- Constant temperature (typically 50-60°F year-round in temperate zones)
- Wind protection
- Rain/snow protection
- Concealment
- Stone reflects fire heat

Cave hazards:
- Bad air: CO2 accumulates, oxygen depletes. Test with flame.
- Falls: cave floors are uneven, wet, and slippery
- Flooding: many caves flood during rain. Look for water marks on walls.
- Animal inhabitants: bats, snakes, spiders, bears
- Collapse: loose rock, especially near entrances
- Disorientation: beyond visual range of entrance, navigation is impossible without light

Safe cave use rules:
1. STAY near the entrance. Never explore deeper than you can see daylight.
2. Fire at the entrance, sleep between fire and back wall.
3. Check ceiling for loose rock before setting up camp.
4. Ensure drainage — even light rain can flood a cavity.
5. Mark the entrance from outside (pile of rocks) so you can find it again.
6. Never enter narrow passages — getting stuck is a fatal scenario.

Cave temperature is your friend: cool in summer, warm (relatively) in winter. This constant temperature reduces exposure risk significantly.

articles.title: Understanding Wildfire Behavior
articles.body:
Knowing how wildfire moves can save your life. Fire is predictable once you understand its physics.

The fire triangle: Heat + Fuel + Oxygen. Remove any one element and fire stops.

How fire spreads:
- Uphill: fire preheats fuel above it via convection. Fire travels uphill 4x faster than on flat ground.
- With wind: wind pushes flame into unburned fuel. A 20mph wind can drive fire at 6+ mph.
- Into dry fuel: dry grass, brush, and pine needles ignite fastest.
- Through spotting: embers carried by wind start new fires ahead of the main front.

Dangerous terrain features:
- Narrow canyons (chimney effect — fire accelerates dramatically)
- Saddles between hills (wind funnels accelerate fire)
- South-facing slopes (dryer fuel, more sun exposure)
- Steep slopes (most dangerous — fire races uphill)

Safe zones:
- Already-burned areas (black)
- Rock fields and gravel
- Wide roads and clearings
- Large water bodies
- Irrigated farmland

Fire behavior changes rapidly with wind shifts. The most dangerous moment is when wind shifts — what was the flank becomes the front, and escape routes become traps.

Rule of thumb: if you can't outrun the fire in 5 minutes, find a safety zone instead of running.
